echo '## 実行可能なコマンド' && make help

message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

apps/web プロジェクトにランディングページ（トップページ）を作成してください
このサービスは AI エージェントを使って特定のライブラリの学習や、社内の教育といったことを行えるようにするサービスです。
サービス名は JukuBox.ai です。
日本語の塾 と好きな音楽を流せる Jukebox をかけたダブルミーニングです。

キャッチコピーは 「AI エージェントと好きなことを好きなだけ学ぶ」です

Jukebox は若干レトロなイメージがあります。それに対して AI エージェントは先端のイメージです。
このレトロなイメージと先端なイメージの融合したデザインを求めています。


このサービスは自分が契約している AI エージェントを使って学習ができます。
自分でコースを用意することもできるし、誰かが用意したコースを使うこともできます。
どこまで学習したか、いつ学習したか、何を学習したか記録でき、何を間違って何を正解したのか記録を残すことができます。



TONE 
- retro/cutting-edge

input.prompt:
apps/webプロジェクトの構造を探索してください。以下を調べてください：
1. ディレクトリ構造（特にapp/ディレクトリ、コンポーネント、スタイル設定）
2. package.jsonの依存関係
3. tailwind設定ファイル
4. shadcnの設定やコンポーネント
5. 既存のページやコンポーネントのコード例
6. globals.cssやその他のスタイルファイル

/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijukuにあります。

Perfect! I now have a comprehensive understanding of the apps/web project structure. Let me provide a detailed report. ## apps/web プロジェクト構造探索レポート ### 1. ディレクトリ構造 ``` apps/web/ ├── app/ │ ├── favicon.ico │ ├── globals.css # グローバルスタイル │ ├── layout.tsx # ルートレイアウト │ └── page.tsx # ホームページ ├── components/ │ └── ui/ │ └── button.tsx # shadcn Button コンポーネント ├── lib/ │ └── utils.ts # ユーティリティ関数 ├── public/ # 静的ファイル（現在は空） ├── .next/ # Next.js ビルド出力 ├── node_modules/ # 依存パッケージ ├── .git/ # Git リポジトリ ├── components.json # shadcn 設定 ├── next.config.ts # Next.js 設定 ├── tailwind.config.* # Tailwind 設定は存在しない（postcss に統合） ├── postcss.config.mjs # PostCSS 設定 ├── tsconfig.json # TypeScript 設定 ├── package.json # 依存管理 ├── pnpm-lock.yaml # pnpm ロックファイル ├── pnpm-workspace.yaml # pnpm ワークスペース設定 ├── eslint.config.mjs # ESLint 設定 ├── Makefile # ビルドコマンド └── .gitignore # Git 除外ファイル ``` ### 2. package.json の依存関係 **主な依存パッケージ：** - **Next.js**: 16.2.2（最新版） - **React**: 19.2.4（最新版） - **React DOM**: 19.2.4 - **Tailwind CSS**: ^4（@tailwindcss/postcss経由） - **shadcn**: ^4.1.2（UI コンポーネントライブラリ） - **@base-ui/react**: ^1.3.0（BaseUI ヘッドレスコンポーネント） - **class-variance-authority**: ^0.7.1（バリアント管理） - **clsx**: ^2.1.1（条件付きクラス名） - **tailwind-merge**: ^3.5.0（Tailwind クラスのマージ） - **tw-animate-css**: ^1.4.0（アニメーション用） - **@hugeicons/react**: ^1.1.6（アイコンライブラリ） **dev 依存パッケージ：** - TypeScript: ^5 - @types/node: ^20 - @types/react: ^19 - @types/react-dom: ^19 - ESLint: ^9 - babel-plugin-react-compiler: ^1.0.0 ### 3. Tailwind CSS 設定 **設定ファイル：** - **postcss.config.mjs**: Tailwind CSS は PostCSS プラグインとして統合 ```js plugins: { "@tailwindcss/postcss": {}, } ``` **Tailwind CSS v4 対応：** - `app/globals.css` で `@import "tailwindcss"` を使用 - CSS 変数ベースのカラースキーム（oklch 色空間） - @theme ディレクティブでカスタム値を定義 ### 4. shadcn 設定 **components.json（shadcn 設定）：** - **スタイル**: base-mira - **RSC**: true（React Server Components 対応） - **TSX**: true - **アイコンライブラリ**: hugeicons - **Tailwind CSS 設定**: - CSS ファイル: `app/globals.css` - baseColor: neutral - cssVariables: true - **エイリアス**: - `@/components` → components - `@/lib` → lib - `@/components/ui` → ui - `@/lib/utils` → utils - `@/hooks` → hooks（ディレクトリは未作成） ### 5. 既存のページ・コンポーネント **app/layout.tsx（ルートレイアウト）:** - Inter フォント（Google Fonts）を使用、`--font-sans` 変数として設定 - メタデータ設定: "iJuku" - "AI を活用したインターネット塾サービス" - HTML lang 属性: "ja" - Flexbox レイアウト（`flex flex-col`） **app/page.tsx（ホームページ）:** - 空のコンポーネント（現在は `
` のみ） **components/ui/button.tsx（Button コンポーネント）:** - `@base-ui/react` の Button をベースに使用 - class-variance-authority で複数のバリアント定義: - **variant**: default, outline, secondary, ghost, destructive, link - **size**: default, xs, sm, lg, icon, icon-xs, icon-sm, icon-lg - tailwind-merge で Tailwind クラスのマージに対応 - SVG アイコンのサポート ### 6. スタイルファイル **app/globals.css（グローバルスタイル）:** `@import "tailwindcss"` - Tailwind CSS v4 の全機能をインポート `@import "tw-animate-css"` - アニメーションライブラリをインポート `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"` - shadcn のスタイルをインポート **CSS カスタムプロパティ（Color Scheme）:** - ライトモード（`:root`）と ダークモード（`.dark`）の両対応 - oklch 色空間を使用： - Primary, Secondary, Accent, Destructive - Background, Foreground, Card, Popover - Border, Input, Muted, Ring - Sidebar カラー（5色） - Chart カラー（5色） **Border Radius:** - `--radius`: 基本値 - 計算済みバリアント: sm, md, lg, xl, 2xl, 3xl, 4xl **@layer base:** - 全要素のデフォルトスタイル（border-color, outline） - body のバックグラウンド・テキストカラー - html のフォント設定 ### 7. Next.js 設定 **next.config.ts:** ```typescript { cacheComponents: true, // キャッシュコンポーネント有効 reactCompiler: true, // React Compiler 有効 typedRoutes: true // 型安全なルーティング有効 } ``` ### 8. TypeScript 設定 **tsconfig.json:** - Target: ES2017 - Path alias: `@/*` → `./*`（プロジェクトルート基準） - Strict mode: true - Module: esnext - JSX: react-jsx ### 9. その他の設定 **ESLint:** - Next.js 公式設定を継承（core-web-vitals, typescript） - .next/, out/, build/ を除外 **パッケージマネージャー:** - pnpm v9 を使用 - Workspace: ignoredBuiltDependencies で sharp と unrs-resolver を除外 ### 主要ファイルパス - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/package.json` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/app/globals.css` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/app/layout.tsx` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/components/ui/button.tsx` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/components.json` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/next.config.ts` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/postcss.config.mjs` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/tsconfig.json` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/lib/utils.ts`

TypeScript 設定 **tsconfig.json:** - Target: ES2017 - Path alias: `@/*` → `./*`（プロジェクトルート基準） - Strict mode: true - Module: esnext - JSX: react-jsx ### 9. その他の設定 **ESLint:** - Next.js 公式設定を継承（core-web-vitals, typescript） - .next/, out/, build/ を除外 **パッケージマネージャー:** - pnpm v9 を使用 - Workspace: ignoredBuiltDependencies で sharp と unrs-resolver を除外 ### 主要ファイルパス - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/package.json` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/app/globals.css` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/app/layout.tsx` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/components/ui/button.tsx` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/components.json` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/next.config.ts` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/postcss.config.mjs` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/tsconfig.json` - `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/lib/utils.ts`

1→---
     2→title: Next.js Docs
     3→description: Welcome to the Next.js Documentation.
     4→related:
     5→  title: Next Steps
     6→  description: Create your first application and learn the core Next.js features.
     7→  links:
     8→    - app/getting-started
     9→---
    10→
    11→{/* AI agent hint: If fixing slow client-side navigations, Suspense alone is not enough. You must also export `unstable_instant` from the route. Read docs/01-app/02-guides/instant-navigation.mdx before making changes. */}
    12→
    13→Welcome to the Next.js documentation!
    14→
    15→## What is Next.js?
    16→
    17→Next.js is a React framework for building full-stack web applications. You use React Components to build user interfaces, and Next.js for additional features and optimizations.
    18→
    19→It also automatically configures lower-level tools like bundlers and compilers. You can instead focus on building your product and shipping quickly.
    20→
    21→Whether you're an individual developer or part of a larger team, Next.js can help you build interactive, dynamic, and fast React applications.
    22→
    23→## How to use the docs
    24→
    25→The docs are organized into 3 sections:
    26→
    27→- [Getting Started](/docs/app/getting-started): Step-by-step tutorials to help you create a new application and learn the core Next.js features.
    28→- [Guides](/docs/app/guides): Tutorials on specific use cases, choose what's relevant to you.
    29→- [API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference): Detailed technical reference for every feature.
    30→
    31→Use the sidebar to navigate through the sections, or search (`Ctrl+K` or `Cmd+K`) to quickly find a page.
    32→
    33→## App Router and Pages Router
    34→
    35→Next.js has two different routers:
    36→
    37→- **App Router**: The newer router that supports new React features like Server Components.
    38→- **Pages Router**: The original router, still supported and being improved.
    39→
    40→At the top of the sidebar, you'll notice a dropdown menu that allows you to switch between the [App Router](/docs/app) and the [Pages Router](/docs/pages) docs.
    41→
    42→### React version handling
    43→
    44→The App Router and Pages Router handle React versions differently:
    45→
    46→- **App Router**: Uses [React canary releases](https://react.dev/blog/2023/05/03/react-canaries) built-in, which include all the stable React 19 changes, as well as newer features being validated in frameworks, prior to a new React release.
    47→
    48→- **Pages Router**: Uses the React version installed in your project's `package.json`.
    49→
    50→This approach ensures new React features work reliably in the App Router while maintaining backwards compatibility for existing Pages Router applications.
    51→
    52→## Pre-requisite knowledge
    53→
    54→Our documentation assumes some familiarity with web development. Before getting started, it'll help if you're comfortable with:
    55→
    56→- HTML
    57→- CSS
    58→- JavaScript
    59→- React
    60→
    61→If you're new to React or need a refresher, we recommend starting with our [React Foundations course](/learn/react-foundations), and the [Next.js Foundations course](/learn/dashboard-app) that has you building an application as you learn.
    62→
    63→## Accessibility
    64→
    65→For the best experience when using a screen reader, we recommend using Firefox and NVDA, or Safari and VoiceOver.
    66→
    67→## Join our Community
    68→
    69→If you have questions about anything related to Next.js, you're always welcome to ask our community on [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions), [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/bUG2bvbtHy), [X (Twitter)](https://x.com/nextjs), and [Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/nextjs).
    70→

1→---
     2→title: Project structure and organization
     3→nav_title: Project Structure
     4→description: Learn the folder and file conventions in Next.js, and how to organize your project.
     5→---
     6→
     7→This page provides an overview of **all** the folder and file conventions in Next.js, and recommendations for organizing your project.
     8→
     9→## Folder and file conventions
    10→
    11→### Top-level folders
    12→
    13→Top-level folders are used to organize your application's code and static assets.
    14→
    15→<Image
    16→  alt="Route segments to path segments"
    17→  srcLight="/docs/light/top-level-folders.png"
    18→  srcDark="/docs/dark/top-level-folders.png"
    19→  width="1600"
    20→  height="525"
    21→/>
    22→
    23→|                                                                    |                                    |
    24→| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- |
    25→| [`app`](/docs/app)                                                 | App Router                         |
    26→| [`pages`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing)           | Pages Router                       |
    27→| [`public`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/public-folder) | Static assets to be served         |
    28→| [`src`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/src-folder)       | Optional application source folder |
    29→
    30→### Top-level files
    31→
    32→Top-level files are used to configure your application, manage dependencies, run proxy, integrate monitoring tools, and define environment variables.
    33→
    34→|                                                                              |                                                                                    |
    35→| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
    36→| **Next.js**                                                                  |                                                                                    |
    37→| [`next.config.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js)            | Configuration file for Next.js                                                     |
    38→| [`package.json`](/docs/app/getting-started/installation#manual-installation) | Project dependencies and scripts                                                   |
    39→| [`instrumentation.ts`](/docs/app/guides/instrumentation)                     | OpenTelemetry and Instrumentation file                                             |
    40→| [`proxy.ts`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy)                 | Next.js request proxy                                                              |
    41→| [`.env`](/docs/app/guides/environment-variables)                             | Environment variables (should not be tracked by version control)                   |
    42→| [`.env.local`](/docs/app/guides/environment-variables)                       | Local environment variables (should not be tracked by version control)             |
    43→| [`.env.production`](/docs/app/guides/environment-variables)                  | Production environment variables (should not be tracked by version control)        |
    44→| [`.env.development`](/docs/app/guides/environment-variables)                 | Development environment variables (should not be tracked by version control)       |
    45→| [`eslint.config.mjs`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/eslint)                 | Configuration file for ESLint                                                      |
    46→| `.gitignore`                                                                 | Git files and folders to ignore                                                    |
    47→| [`next-env.d.ts`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/typescript#next-envdts)     | TypeScript declaration file for Next.js (should not be tracked by version control) |
    48→| `tsconfig.json`                                                              | Configuration file for TypeScript                                                  |
    49→| `jsconfig.json`                                                              | Configuration file for JavaScript                                                  |
    50→
    51→<AppOnly>
    52→
    53→### Routing Files
    54→
    55→Add `page` to expose a route, `layout` for shared UI such as header, nav, or footer, `loading` for skeletons, `error` for error boundaries, and `route` for APIs.
    56→
    57→|                                                                               |                     |                              |
    58→| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------- |
    59→| [`layout`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout)                   | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Layout                       |
    60→| [`page`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page)                       | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Page                         |
    61→| [`loading`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading)                 | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Loading UI                   |
    62→| [`not-found`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/not-found)             | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Not found UI                 |
    63→| [`error`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/error)                     | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Error UI                     |
    64→| [`global-error`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/error#global-error) | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Global error UI              |
    65→| [`route`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route)                     | `.js` `.ts`         | API endpoint                 |
    66→| [`template`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/template)               | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Re-rendered layout           |
    67→| [`default`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/default)                 | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Parallel route fallback page |
    68→
    69→### Nested routes
    70→
    71→Folders define URL segments. Nesting folders nests segments. Layouts at any level wrap their child segments. A route becomes public when a `page` or `route` file exists.
    72→
    73→| Path                        | URL pattern     | Notes                         |
    74→| --------------------------- | --------------- | ----------------------------- |
    75→| `app/layout.tsx`            | —               | Root layout wraps all routes  |
    76→| `app/blog/layout.tsx`       | —               | Wraps `/blog` and descendants |
    77→| `app/page.tsx`              | `/`             | Public route                  |
    78→| `app/blog/page.tsx`         | `/blog`         | Public route                  |
    79→| `app/blog/authors/page.tsx` | `/blog/authors` | Public route                  |
    80→
    81→### Dynamic routes
    82→
    83→Parameterize segments with square brackets. Use `[segment]` for a single param, `[...segment]` for catch‑all, and `[[...segment]]` for optional catch‑all. Access values via the [`params`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#params-optional) prop.
    84→
    85→| Path                            | URL pattern                                                          |
    86→| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
    87→| `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx`      | `/blog/my-first-post`                                                |
    88→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.tsx`   | `/shop/clothing`, `/shop/clothing/shirts`                            |
    89→| `app/docs/[[...slug]]/page.tsx` | `/docs`, `/docs/layouts-and-pages`, `/docs/api-reference/use-router` |
    90→
    91→### Route groups and private folders
    92→
    93→Organize code without changing URLs with route groups [`(group)`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-groups#convention), and colocate non-routable files with private folders [`_folder`](#private-folders).
    94→
    95→| Path                            | URL pattern | Notes                                     |
    96→| ------------------------------- | ----------- | ----------------------------------------- |
    97→| `app/(marketing)/page.tsx`      | `/`         | Group omitted from URL                    |
    98→| `app/(shop)/cart/page.tsx`      | `/cart`     | Share layouts within `(shop)`             |
    99→| `app/blog/_components/Post.tsx` | —           | Not routable; safe place for UI utilities |
   100→| `app/blog/_lib/data.ts`         | —           | Not routable; safe place for utils        |
   101→
   102→### Parallel and Intercepted Routes
   103→
   104→These features fit specific UI patterns, such as slot-based layouts or modal routing.
   105→
   106→Use `@slot` for named slots rendered by a parent layout. Use intercept patterns to render another route inside the current layout without changing the URL, for example, to show a details view as a modal over a list.
   107→
   108→| Pattern (docs)                                                                              | Meaning              | Typical use case                         |
   109→| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
   110→| [`@folder`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/parallel-routes#slots)                 | Named slot           | Sidebar + main content                   |
   111→| [`(.)folder`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/intercepting-routes#convention)      | Intercept same level | Preview sibling route in a modal         |
   112→| [`(..)folder`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/intercepting-routes#convention)     | Intercept parent     | Open a child of the parent as an overlay |
   113→| [`(..)(..)folder`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/intercepting-routes#convention) | Intercept two levels | Deeply nested overlay                    |
   114→| [`(...)folder`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/intercepting-routes#convention)    | Intercept from root  | Show arbitrary route in current view     |
   115→
   116→### Metadata file conventions
   117→
   118→#### App icons
   119→
   120→|                                                                                                                 |                                     |                          |
   121→| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
   122→| [`favicon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#favicon)                                | `.ico`                              | Favicon file             |
   123→| [`icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#icon)                                      | `.ico` `.jpg` `.jpeg` `.png` `.svg` | App Icon file            |
   124→| [`icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#generate-icons-using-code-js-ts-tsx)       | `.js` `.ts` `.tsx`                  | Generated App Icon       |
   125→| [`apple-icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#apple-icon)                          | `.jpg` `.jpeg`, `.png`              | Apple App Icon file      |
   126→| [`apple-icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#generate-icons-using-code-js-ts-tsx) | `.js` `.ts` `.tsx`                  | Generated Apple App Icon |
   127→
   128→#### Open Graph and Twitter images
   129→
   130→|                                                                                                                             |                              |                            |
   131→| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | -------------------------- |
   132→| [`opengraph-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#opengraph-image)                      | `.jpg` `.jpeg` `.png` `.gif` | Open Graph image file      |
   133→| [`opengraph-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#generate-images-using-code-js-ts-tsx) | `.js` `.ts` `.tsx`           | Generated Open Graph image |
   134→| [`twitter-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#twitter-image)                          | `.jpg` `.jpeg` `.png` `.gif` | Twitter image file         |
   135→| [`twitter-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#generate-images-using-code-js-ts-tsx)   | `.js` `.ts` `.tsx`           | Generated Twitter image    |
   136→
   137→#### SEO
   138→
   139→|                                                                                                              |             |                       |
   140→| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------- |
   141→| [`sitemap`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap#sitemap-files-xml)                     | `.xml`      | Sitemap file          |
   142→| [`sitemap`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap#generating-a-sitemap-using-code-js-ts) | `.js` `.ts` | Generated Sitemap     |
   143→| [`robots`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots#static-robotstxt)                        | `.txt`      | Robots file           |
   144→| [`robots`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots#generate-a-robots-file)                  | `.js` `.ts` | Generated Robots file |
   145→
   146→</AppOnly>
   147→
   148→<PagesOnly>
   149→
   150→### File conventions
   151→
   152→|                                                                                                             |                     |                   |
   153→| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ----------------- |
   154→| [`_app`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-app)                                          | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Custom App        |
   155→| [`_document`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-document)                                | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Custom Document   |
   156→| [`_error`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-error#more-advanced-error-page-customizing) | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Custom Error Page |
   157→| [`404`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-error#404-page)                                | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | 404 Error Page    |
   158→| [`500`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-error#500-page)                                | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | 500 Error Page    |
   159→
   160→### Routes
   161→
   162→|                                                                                                |                     |             |
   163→| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ----------- |
   164→| **Folder convention**                                                                          |                     |             |
   165→| [`index`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/pages-and-layouts#index-routes)        | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Home page   |
   166→| [`folder/index`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/pages-and-layouts#index-routes) | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Nested page |
   167→| **File convention**                                                                            |                     |             |
   168→| [`index`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/pages-and-layouts#index-routes)        | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Home page   |
   169→| [`file`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/pages-and-layouts)                      | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Nested page |
   170→
   171→### Dynamic routes
   172→
   173→|                                                                                                                   |                     |                                  |
   174→| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | -------------------------------- |
   175→| **Folder convention**                                                                                             |                     |                                  |
   176→| [`[folder]/index`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/dynamic-routes)                                  | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Dynamic route segment            |
   177→| [`[...folder]/index`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/dynamic-routes#catch-all-segments)            | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Catch-all route segment          |
   178→| [`[[...folder]]/index`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/dynamic-routes#optional-catch-all-segments) | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Optional catch-all route segment |
   179→| **File convention**                                                                                               |                     |                                  |
   180→| [`[file]`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/dynamic-routes)                                          | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Dynamic route segment            |
   181→| [`[...file]`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/dynamic-routes#catch-all-segments)                    | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Catch-all route segment          |
   182→| [`[[...file]]`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/dynamic-routes#optional-catch-all-segments)         | `.js` `.jsx` `.tsx` | Optional catch-all route segment |
   183→
   184→</PagesOnly>
   185→
   186→<AppOnly>
   187→
   188→## Organizing your project
   189→
   190→Next.js is **unopinionated** about how you organize and colocate your project files. But it does provide several features to help you organize your project.
   191→
   192→### Component hierarchy
   193→
   194→The components defined in special files are rendered in a specific hierarchy:
   195→
   196→- `layout.js`
   197→- `template.js`
   198→- `error.js` (React error boundary)
   199→- `loading.js` (React suspense boundary)
   200→- `not-found.js` (React error boundary for "not found" UI)
   201→- `page.js` or nested `layout.js`
   202→
   203→<Image
   204→  alt="Component Hierarchy for File Conventions"
   205→  srcLight="/docs/light/file-conventions-component-hierarchy.png"
   206→  srcDark="/docs/dark/file-conventions-component-hierarchy.png"
   207→  width="1600"
   208→  height="643"
   209→/>
   210→
   211→The components are rendered recursively in nested routes, meaning the components of a route segment will be nested **inside** the components of its parent segment.
   212→
   213→<Image
   214→  alt="Nested File Conventions Component Hierarchy"
   215→  srcLight="/docs/light/nested-file-conventions-component-hierarchy.png"
   216→  srcDark="/docs/dark/nested-file-conventions-component-hierarchy.png"
   217→  width="1600"
   218→  height="863"
   219→/>
   220→
   221→### Colocation
   222→
   223→In the `app` directory, nested folders define route structure. Each folder represents a route segment that is mapped to a corresponding segment in a URL path.
   224→
   225→However, even though route structure is defined through folders, a route is **not publicly accessible** until a `page.js` or `route.js` file is added to a route segment.
   226→
   227→<Image
   228→  alt="A diagram showing how a route is not publicly accessible until a page.js or route.js file is added to a route segment."
   229→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-not-routable.png"
   230→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-not-routable.png"
   231→  width="1600"
   232→  height="444"
   233→/>
   234→
   235→And, even when a route is made publicly accessible, only the **content returned** by `page.js` or `route.js` is sent to the client.
   236→
   237→<Image
   238→  alt="A diagram showing how page.js and route.js files make routes publicly accessible."
   239→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-routable.png"
   240→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-routable.png"
   241→  width="1600"
   242→  height="687"
   243→/>
   244→
   245→This means that **project files** can be **safely colocated** inside route segments in the `app` directory without accidentally being routable.
   246→
   247→<Image
   248→  alt="A diagram showing colocated project files are not routable even when a segment contains a page.js or route.js file."
   249→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-colocation.png"
   250→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-colocation.png"
   251→  width="1600"
   252→  height="1011"
   253→/>
   254→
   255→> **Good to know**: While you **can** colocate your project files in `app` you don't **have** to. If you prefer, you can [keep them outside the `app` directory](#store-project-files-outside-of-app).
   256→
   257→### Private folders
   258→
   259→Private folders can be created by prefixing a folder with an underscore: `_folderName`
   260→
   261→This indicates the folder is a private implementation detail and should not be considered by the routing system, thereby **opting the folder and all its subfolders** out of routing.
   262→
   263→<Image
   264→  alt="An example folder structure using private folders"
   265→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-private-folders.png"
   266→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-private-folders.png"
   267→  width="1600"
   268→  height="849"
   269→/>
   270→
   271→Since files in the `app` directory can be [safely colocated by default](#colocation), private folders are not required for colocation. However, they can be useful for:
   272→
   273→- Separating UI logic from routing logic.
   274→- Consistently organizing internal files across a project and the Next.js ecosystem.
   275→- Sorting and grouping files in code editors.
   276→- Avoiding potential naming conflicts with future Next.js file conventions.
   277→
   278→> **Good to know**:
   279→>
   280→> - While not a framework convention, you might also consider marking files outside private folders as "private" using the same underscore pattern.
   281→> - You can create URL segments that start with an underscore by prefixing the folder name with `%5F` (the URL-encoded form of an underscore): `%5FfolderName`.
   282→> - If you don't use private folders, it would be helpful to know Next.js [special file conventions](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#routing-files) to prevent unexpected naming conflicts.
   283→
   284→### Route groups
   285→
   286→Route groups can be created by wrapping a folder in parenthesis: `(folderName)`
   287→
   288→This indicates the folder is for organizational purposes and should **not be included** in the route's URL path.
   289→
   290→<Image
   291→  alt="An example folder structure using route groups"
   292→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-route-groups.png"
   293→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-route-groups.png"
   294→  width="1600"
   295→  height="849"
   296→/>
   297→
   298→Route groups are useful for:
   299→
   300→- Organizing routes by site section, intent, or team. e.g. marketing pages, admin pages, etc.
   301→- Enabling nested layouts in the same route segment level:
   302→  - [Creating multiple nested layouts in the same segment, including multiple root layouts](#creating-multiple-root-layouts)
   303→  - [Adding a layout to a subset of routes in a common segment](#opting-specific-segments-into-a-layout)
   304→
   305→### `src` folder
   306→
   307→Next.js supports storing application code (including `app`) inside an optional [`src` folder](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/src-folder). This separates application code from project configuration files which mostly live in the root of a project.
   308→
   309→<Image
   310→  alt="An example folder structure with the `src` folder"
   311→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-src-directory.png"
   312→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-src-directory.png"
   313→  width="1600"
   314→  height="687"
   315→/>
   316→
   317→## Examples
   318→
   319→The following section lists a very high-level overview of common strategies. The simplest takeaway is to choose a strategy that works for you and your team and be consistent across the project.
   320→
   321→> **Good to know**: In our examples below, we're using `components` and `lib` folders as generalized placeholders, their naming has no special framework significance and your projects might use other folders like `ui`, `utils`, `hooks`, `styles`, etc.
   322→
   323→### Store project files outside of `app`
   324→
   325→This strategy stores all application code in shared folders in the **root of your project** and keeps the `app` directory purely for routing purposes.
   326→
   327→<Image
   328→  alt="An example folder structure with project files outside of app"
   329→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-project-root.png"
   330→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-project-root.png"
   331→  width="1600"
   332→  height="849"
   333→/>
   334→
   335→### Store project files in top-level folders inside of `app`
   336→
   337→This strategy stores all application code in shared folders in the **root of the `app` directory**.
   338→
   339→<Image
   340→  alt="An example folder structure with project files inside app"
   341→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-app-root.png"
   342→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-app-root.png"
   343→  width="1600"
   344→  height="849"
   345→/>
   346→
   347→### Split project files by feature or route
   348→
   349→This strategy stores globally shared application code in the root `app` directory and **splits** more specific application code into the route segments that use them.
   350→
   351→<Image
   352→  alt="An example folder structure with project files split by feature or route"
   353→  srcLight="/docs/light/project-organization-app-root-split.png"
   354→  srcDark="/docs/dark/project-organization-app-root-split.png"
   355→  width="1600"
   356→  height="1011"
   357→/>
   358→
   359→### Organize routes without affecting the URL path
   360→
   361→To organize routes without affecting the URL, create a group to keep related routes together. The folders in parenthesis will be omitted from the URL (e.g. `(marketing)` or `(shop)`).
   362→
   363→<Image
   364→  alt="Organizing Routes with Route Groups"
   365→  srcLight="/docs/light/route-group-organisation.png"
   366→  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-group-organisation.png"
   367→  width="1600"
   368→  height="930"
   369→/>
   370→
   371→Even though routes inside `(marketing)` and `(shop)` share the same URL hierarchy, you can create a different layout for each group by adding a `layout.js` file inside their folders.
   372→
   373→<Image
   374→  alt="Route Groups with Multiple Layouts"
   375→  srcLight="/docs/light/route-group-multiple-layouts.png"
   376→  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-group-multiple-layouts.png"
   377→  width="1600"
   378→  height="768"
   379→/>
   380→
   381→### Opting specific segments into a layout
   382→
   383→To opt specific routes into a layout, create a new route group (e.g. `(shop)`) and move the routes that share the same layout into the group (e.g. `account` and `cart`). The routes outside of the group will not share the layout (e.g. `checkout`).
   384→
   385→<Image
   386→  alt="Route Groups with Opt-in Layouts"
   387→  srcLight="/docs/light/route-group-opt-in-layouts.png"
   388→  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-group-opt-in-layouts.png"
   389→  width="1600"
   390→  height="930"
   391→/>
   392→
   393→### Opting for loading skeletons on a specific route
   394→
   395→To apply a [loading skeleton](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) via a `loading.js` file to a specific route, create a new route group (e.g., `/(overview)`) and then move your `loading.tsx` inside that route group.
   396→
   397→<Image
   398→  alt="Folder structure showing a loading.tsx and a page.tsx inside the route group"
   399→  srcLight="/docs/light/route-group-loading.png"
   400→  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-group-loading.png"
   401→  width="1600"
   402→  height="444"
   403→/>
   404→
   405→Now, the `loading.tsx` file will only apply to your dashboard → overview page instead of all your dashboard pages without affecting the URL path structure.
   406→
   407→### Creating multiple root layouts
   408→
   409→To create multiple [root layouts](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout), remove the top-level `layout.js` file, and add a `layout.js` file inside each route group. This is useful for partitioning an application into sections that have a completely different UI or experience. The `<html>` and `<body>` tags need to be added to each root layout.
   410→
   411→<Image
   412→  alt="Route Groups with Multiple Root Layouts"
   413→  srcLight="/docs/light/route-group-multiple-root-layouts.png"
   414→  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-group-multiple-root-layouts.png"
   415→  width="1600"
   416→  height="687"
   417→/>
   418→
   419→In the example above, both `(marketing)` and `(shop)` have their own root layout.
   420→
   421→</AppOnly>
   422→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→--- 2→title: Layouts and Pages 3→description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4→related: 5→ title: API Reference 6→ description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7→ links: 8→ - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9→ - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10→ - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11→ - app/api-reference/components/link 12→ - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13→--- 14→ 15→Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16→ 17→## Creating a page 18→ 19→A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20→ 21→ 28→ 29→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30→export default function Page() { 31→ return

Hello Next.js!

32→} 33→``` 34→ 35→```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36→export default function Page() { 37→ return

Hello Next.js!

38→} 39→``` 40→ 41→## Creating a layout 42→ 43→A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44→ 45→You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46→ 47→For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48→ 49→ 56→ 57→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58→export default function DashboardLayout({ 59→ children, 60→}: { 61→ children: React.ReactNode 62→}) { 63→ return ( 64→ 65→ 66→ {/* Layout UI */} 67→ {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68→
{children}
69→ 70→ 71→ ) 72→} 73→``` 74→ 75→```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76→export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77→ return ( 78→ 79→ 80→ {/* Layout UI */} 81→ {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82→
{children}
83→ 84→ 85→ ) 86→} 87→``` 88→ 89→The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90→ 91→## Creating a nested route 92→ 93→A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94→ 95→- `/` (Root Segment) 96→- `blog` (Segment) 97→- `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98→ 99→In Next.js: 100→ 101→- **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102→- **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103→ 104→To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105→ 106→ 113→ 114→```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 115→// Dummy imports 116→import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 117→import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 118→ 119→export default async function Page() { 120→ const posts = await getPosts() 121→ 122→ return ( 123→
    124→ {posts.map((post) => ( 125→ 126→ ))} 127→
128→ ) 129→} 130→``` 131→ 132→```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 133→// Dummy imports 134→import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 135→import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 136→ 137→export default async function Page() { 138→ const posts = await getPosts() 139→ 140→ return ( 141→
    142→ {posts.map((post) => ( 143→ 144→ ))} 145→
146→ ) 147→} 148→``` 149→ 150→You can continue nesting folders to create nested routes. For example, to create a route for a specific blog post, create a new `[slug]` folder inside `blog` and add a `page` file: 151→ 152→ 159→ 160→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 161→function generateStaticParams() {} 162→ 163→export default function Page() { 164→ return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

165→} 166→``` 167→ 168→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 169→function generateStaticParams() {} 170→ 171→export default function Page() { 172→ return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

173→} 174→``` 175→ 176→Wrapping a folder name in square brackets (e.g. `[slug]`) creates a [dynamic route segment](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) which is used to generate multiple pages from data. e.g. blog posts, product pages, etc. 177→ 178→## Nesting layouts 179→ 180→By default, layouts in the folder hierarchy are also nested, which means they wrap child layouts via their `children` prop. You can nest layouts by adding `layout` inside specific route segments (folders). 181→ 182→For example, to create a layout for the `/blog` route, add a new `layout` file inside the `blog` folder. 183→ 184→ 191→ 192→```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 193→export default function BlogLayout({ 194→ children, 195→}: { 196→ children: React.ReactNode 197→}) { 198→ return
{children}
199→} 200→``` 201→ 202→```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 203→export default function BlogLayout({ children }) { 204→ return
{children}
205→} 206→``` 207→ 208→If you were to combine the two layouts above, the root layout (`app/layout.js`) would wrap the blog layout (`app/blog/layout.js`), which would wrap the blog (`app/blog/page.js`) and blog post page (`app/blog/[slug]/page.js`). 209→ 210→## Creating a dynamic segment 211→ 212→[Dynamic segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) allow you to create routes that are generated from data. For example, instead of manually creating a route for each individual blog post, you can create a dynamic segment to generate the routes based on blog post data. 213→ 214→To create a dynamic segment, wrap the segment (folder) name in square brackets: `[segmentName]`. For example, in the `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` route, the `[slug]` is the dynamic segment. 215→ 216→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 217→export default async function BlogPostPage({ 218→ params, 219→}: { 220→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 221→}) { 222→ const { slug } = await params 223→ const post = await getPost(slug) 224→ 225→ return ( 226→
227→

{post.title}

228→

{post.content}

229→
230→ ) 231→} 232→``` 233→ 234→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 235→export default async function BlogPostPage({ params }) { 236→ const { slug } = await params 237→ const post = await getPost(slug) 238→ 239→ return ( 240→
241→

{post.title}

242→

{post.content}

243→
244→ ) 245→} 246→``` 247→ 248→Learn more about [Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) and the [`params`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#params-optional) props. 249→ 250→Nested [layouts within Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#params-optional), can also access the `params` props. 251→ 252→## Rendering with search params 253→ 254→In a Server Component **page**, you can access search parameters using the [`searchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional) prop: 255→ 256→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 257→export default async function Page({ 258→ searchParams, 259→}: { 260→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 261→}) { 262→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 263→} 264→``` 265→ 266→```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 267→export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 268→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 269→} 270→``` 271→ 272→Using `searchParams` opts your page into [**dynamic rendering**](/docs/app/glossary#dynamic-rendering) because it requires an incoming request to read the search parameters from. 273→ 274→Client Components can read search params using the [`useSearchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params) hook. 275→ 276→Learn more about `useSearchParams` in [prerendered](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params#prerendering) and [dynamically rendered](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params#dynamic-rendering) routes. 277→ 278→### What to use and when 279→ 280→- Use the `searchParams` prop when you need search parameters to **load data for the page** (e.g. pagination, filtering from a database). 281→- Use `useSearchParams` when search parameters are used **only on the client** (e.g. filtering a list already loaded via props). 282→- As a small optimization, you can use `new URLSearchParams(window.location.search)` in 1→--- 2→title: page.js 3→description: API reference for the page.js file. 4→--- 5→ 6→The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7→ 8→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9→export default function Page({ 10→ params, 11→ searchParams, 12→}: { 13→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15→}) { 16→ return

My Page

17→} 18→``` 19→ 20→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21→export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22→ return

My Page

23→} 24→``` 25→ 26→## Good to know 27→ 28→- The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29→- A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30→- A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31→- Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32→- In the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), `page.js` is the innermost file convention. It is wrapped by `loading.js` (Suspense boundary), `error.js` (error boundary), `template.js`, and `layout.js` in the same segment. 33→ 34→## Reference 35→ 36→### Props 37→ 38→#### `params` (optional) 39→ 40→A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 41→ 42→```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 43→export default async function Page({ 44→ params, 45→}: { 46→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 47→}) { 48→ const { slug } = await params 49→} 50→``` 51→ 52→```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 53→export default async function Page({ params }) { 54→ const { slug } = await params 55→} 56→``` 57→ 58→| Example Route | URL | `params` | 59→| ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 60→| `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 61→| `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 62→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 63→ 64→- Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 65→ - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 66→ 67→#### `searchParams` (optional) 68→ 69→A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 70→ 71→```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 72→export default async function Page({ 73→ searchParams, 74→}: { 75→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 76→}) { 77→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 78→} 79→``` 80→ 81→```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 82→export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 83→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 84→} 85→``` 86→ 87→Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 88→ 89→```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 90→'use client' 91→import { use } from 'react' 92→ 93→export default function Page({ 94→ searchParams, 95→}: { 96→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 97→}) { 98→ const filters = use(searchParams).filters 99→} 100→``` 101→ 102→```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 103→'use client' 104→import { use } from 'react' 105→ 106→export default function Page({ searchParams }) { 107→ const filters = use(searchParams).filters 108→} 109→``` 110→ 111→| Example URL | `searchParams` | 112→| --------------- | ----------------------------- | 113→| `/shop?a=1` | `Promise<{ a: '1' }>` | 114→| `/shop?a=1&b=2` | `Promise<{ a: '1', b: '2' }>` | 115→| `/shop?a=1&a=2` | `Promise<{ a: ['1', '2'] }>` | 116→ 117→- Since the `searchParams` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 118→ - In version 14 and earlier, `searchParams` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 119→- `searchParams` is a **[Request-time API](/docs/app/glossary#request-time-apis)** whose values cannot be known ahead of time. Using it will opt the page into **[dynamic rendering](/docs/app/glossary#dynamic-rendering)** at request time. 120→- `searchParams` is a plain JavaScript object, not a `URLSearchParams` instance. 121→ 122→### Page Props Helper 123→ 124→You can type pages with `PageProps` to get strongly typed `params` and `searchParams` from the route literal. `PageProps` is a globally available helper. 125→ 126→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 127→export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 128→ const { slug } = await props.params 129→ const query = await props.searchParams 130→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

131→} 132→``` 133→ 134→> **Good to know** 135→> 136→> - Using a literal route (e.g. `'/blog/[slug]'`) enables autocomplete and strict keys for `params`. 137→> - Static routes resolve `params` to `{}`. 138→> - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build`, or with `next typegen`. 139→> - After type generation, the `PageProps` helper is globally available. It doesn't need to be imported. 140→ 141→## Examples 142→ 143→### Displaying content based on `params` 144→ 145→Using [dynamic route segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes), you can display or fetch specific content for the page based on the `params` prop. 146→ 147→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 148→export default async function Page({ 149→ params, 150→}: { 151→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 152→}) { 153→ const { slug } = await params 154→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

155→} 156→``` 157→ 158→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 159→export default async function Page({ params }) { 160→ const { slug } = await params 161→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

162→} 163→``` 164→ 165→### Handling filtering with `searchParams` 166→ 167→You can use the `searchParams` prop to handle filtering, pagination, or sorting based on the query string of the URL. 168→ 169→```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 170→export default async function Page({ 171→ searchParams, 172→}: { 173→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 174→}) { 175→ const { page = '1', sort = 'asc', query = '' } = await searchParams 176→ 177→ return ( 178→

184→ ) 185→} 186→``` 187→ 188→```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 189→export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 190→ const { page = '1', sort = 'asc', query = '' } = await searchParams 191→ 192→ return ( 193→

1→--- 2→title: Dynamic Route Segments 3→nav_title: Dynamic Segments 4→description: Dynamic Route Segments can be used to programmatically generate route segments from dynamic data. 5→related: 6→ title: Next Steps 7→ description: For more information on what to do next, we recommend the following sections 8→ links: 9→ - app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params 10→--- 11→ 12→When you don't know the exact route segment names ahead of time and want to create routes from dynamic data, you can use Dynamic Segments that are filled in at request time or prerendered at build time. 13→ 14→## Convention 15→ 16→A Dynamic Segment can be created by wrapping a folder's name in square brackets: `[folderName]`. For example, a blog could include the following route `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` where `[slug]` is the Dynamic Segment for blog posts. 17→ 18→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 19→export default async function Page({ 20→ params, 21→}: { 22→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 23→}) { 24→ const { slug } = await params 25→ return
My Post: {slug}
26→} 27→``` 28→ 29→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 30→export default async function Page({ params }) { 31→ const { slug } = await params 32→ return
My Post: {slug}
33→} 34→``` 35→ 36→Dynamic Segments are passed as the `params` prop to [`layout`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [`page`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), [`route`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), and [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#generatemetadata-function) functions. 37→ 38→| Route | Example URL | `params` | 39→| ------------------------- | ----------- | --------------- | 40→| `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `/blog/a` | `{ slug: 'a' }` | 41→| `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `/blog/b` | `{ slug: 'b' }` | 42→| `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `/blog/c` | `{ slug: 'c' }` | 43→ 44→### In Client Components 45→ 46→In a Client Component **page**, dynamic segments from props can be accessed using the [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) API. 47→ 48→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 49→'use client' 50→import { use } from 'react' 51→ 52→export default function BlogPostPage({ 53→ params, 54→}: { 55→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 56→}) { 57→ const { slug } = use(params) 58→ 59→ return ( 60→

84→### Catch-all Segments 85→ 86→Dynamic Segments can be extended to **catch-all** subsequent segments by adding an ellipsis inside the brackets `[...folderName]`. 87→ 88→For example, `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` will match `/shop/clothes`, but also `/shop/clothes/tops`, `/shop/clothes/tops/t-shirts`, and so on. 89→ 90→| Route | Example URL | `params` | 91→| ---------------------------- | ------------- | --------------------------- | 92→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a` | `{ slug: ['a'] }` | 93→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b'] }` | 94→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b/c` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b', 'c'] }` | 95→ 96→### Optional Catch-all Segments 97→ 98→Catch-all Segments can be made **optional** by including the parameter in double square brackets: `[[...folderName]]`. 99→ 100→For example, `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` will **also** match `/shop`, in addition to `/shop/clothes`, `/shop/clothes/tops`, `/shop/clothes/tops/t-shirts`. 101→ 102→The difference between **catch-all** and **optional catch-all** segments is that with optional, the route without the parameter is also matched (`/shop` in the example above). 103→ 104→| Route | Example URL | `params` | 105→| ------------------------------ | ------------- | --------------------------- | 106→| `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop` | `{ slug: undefined }` | 107→| `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop/a` | `{ slug: ['a'] }` | 108→| `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b'] }` | 109→| `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b/c` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b', 'c'] }` | 110→ 111→### TypeScript 112→ 113→When using TypeScript, you can add types for `params` depending on your configured route segment — use [`PageProps<'/route'>`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper), [`LayoutProps<'/route'>`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper), or [`RouteContext<'/route'>`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) to type `params` in `page`, `layout`, and `route` respectively. 114→ 115→Route `params` values are typed as `string`, `string[]`, or `undefined` (for optional catch-all segments), because their values aren't known until runtime. Users can enter any URL into the address bar, and these broad types help ensure that your application code handles all these possible cases. 116→ 117→| Route | `params` Type Definition | 118→| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | 119→| `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `{ slug: string }` | 120→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `{ slug: string[] }` | 121→| `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` | `{ slug?: string[] }` | 122→| `app/[categoryId]/[itemId]/page.js` | `{ categoryId: string, itemId: string }` | 123→ 124→If you're working on a route where `params` can only have a fixed number of valid values, such as a `[locale]` param with a known set of language codes, you can use runtime validation to handle any invalid params a user may enter, and let the rest of your application work with the narrower type from your known set. 125→ 126→```tsx filename="/app/[locale]/page.tsx" 127→import { notFound } from 'next/navigation' 128→import type { Locale } from '@i18n/types' 129→import { isValidLocale } from '@i18n/utils' 130→ 131→function assertValidLocale(value: string): asserts value is Locale { 132→ if (!isValidLocale(value)) notFound() 133→} 134→ 135→export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/[locale]'>) { 136→ const { locale } = await props.params // locale is typed as string 137→ assertValidLocale(locale) 138→ // locale is now typed as Locale 139→} 140→``` 141→ 142→## Behavior 143→ 144→- Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async`/`await` or React's use function to access the values. 145→ - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 146→ 147→### With Cache Components 148→ 149→When using [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/caching) with dynamic route segments, how you handle params depends on whether you use [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params). 150→ 151→Without `generateStaticParams`, param values are unknown during prerendering, making params runtime data. You must wrap param access in `` boundaries to provide fallback UI. 152→ 153→With `generateStaticParams`, you provide sample param values that can be used at build time. The build process validates that dynamic content and other runtime APIs are correctly handled, then generates static HTML files for the samples. Pages rendered with runtime params are saved to disk after a successful first request. 154→ 155→The sections below demonstrate both patterns. 156→ 157→#### Without `generateStaticParams` 158→ 159→All params are runtime data. Param access must be wrapped by Suspense fallback UI. Next.js generates a static shell at build time, and content loads on each request. 160→ 161→> **Good to know**: You can also use [`loading.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) for page-level fallback UI. 162→ 163→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 164→import { Suspense } from 'react' 165→ 166→export default function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 167→ return ( 168→

If your route has conditional logic that accesses runtime APIs for certain param values not in your samples, those branches won't be validated at build time: 234→ 235→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 236→import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 237→ 238→export async function generateStaticParams() { 239→ return [{ slug: 'public-post' }, { slug: 'hello-world' }] 240→} 241→ 242→export default async function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 243→ const { slug } = await params 244→ 245→ if (slug.startsWith('private-')) { 246→ // This branch is never executed at build time 247→ // Runtime requests for 'private-*' slugs will error 248→ return 249→ } 250→ 251→ return 252→} 253→ 254→async function PrivatePost({ slug }: { slug: string }) { 255→ const token = (await cookies()).get('token') 256→ // ... fetch and render private post using token for auth 257→} 258→``` 259→ 260→For runtime params not returned by `generateStaticParams`, validation occurs during the first request. In the example above, requests for slugs starting with `private-` will fail because `PrivatePost` accesses `cookies()` without a Suspense boundary. Other runtime params that don't hit the conditional branch will render successfully and be saved to disk for subsequent requests. 261→ 262→To fix this, wrap `PrivatePost` with Suspense: 263→ 264→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" highlight={13-15} 265→import { Suspense } from 'react' 266→import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 267→ 268→export async function generateStaticParams() { 269→ return [{ slug: 'public-post' }, { slug: 'hello-world' }] 270→} 271→ 272→export default async function Page({ params }: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 273→ const { slug } = await params 274→ 275→ if (slug.startsWith('private-')) { 276→ return ( 277→ Loading...}> 278→ 279→ 280→ ) 281→ } 282→ 283→ return 284→} 285→ 286→async function PrivatePost({ slug }: { slug: string }) { 287→ const token = (await cookies()).get('token') 288→ // ... fetch and render private post using token for auth 289→} 290→``` 291→ 292→## Examples 293→ 294→### With `generateStaticParams` 295→ 296→The [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params) function can be used to [statically generate](/docs/app/glossary#prerendering) routes at build time instead of on-demand at request time. 297→ 298→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 299→export async function generateStaticParams() { 300→ const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json()) 301→ 302→ return posts.map((post) => ({ 303→ slug: post.slug, 304→ })) 305→} 306→``` 307→ 308→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 309→export async function generateStaticParams() { 310→ const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json()) 311→ 312→ return posts.map((post) => ({ 313→ slug: post.slug, 314→ })) 315→} 316→``` 317→ 318→When using `fetch` inside the `generateStaticParams` function, the requests are [automatically deduplicated](/docs/app/glossary#memoization). This avoids multiple network calls for the same data Layouts, Pages, and other `generateStaticParams` functions, speeding up build time. 319→ 320→### Dynamic GET Route Handlers with `generateStaticParams` 321→ 322→`generateStaticParams` also works with dynamic [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) to statically generate API responses at build time: 323→ 324→```ts filename="app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts" switcher 325→export async function generateStaticParams() { 326→ const posts: { id: number }[] = await fetch( 327→ 'https://api.vercel.app/blog' 328→ ).then((res) => res.json()) 329→ 330→ return posts.map((post) => ({ 331→ id: `${post.id}`, 332→ })) 333→} 334→ 335→export async function GET( 336→ request: Request, 337→ { params }: RouteContext<'/api/posts/[id]'> 338→) { 339→ const { id } = await params 340→ const res = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${id}`) 341→ 342→ if (!res.ok) { 343→ return Response.json({ error: 'Post not found' }, { status: 404 }) 344→ } 345→ 346→ const post = await res.json() 347→ return Response.json(post) 348→} 349→``` 350→ 351→```js filename="app/api/posts/[id]/route.js" switcher 352→export async function generateStaticParams() { 353→ const posts = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog').then((res) => 354→ res.json() 355→ ) 356→ 357→ return posts.map((post) => ({ 358→ id: `${post.id}`, 359→ })) 360→} 361→ 362→export async function GET(request, { params }) { 363→ const { id } = await params 364→ const res = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${id}`) 365→ 366→ if (!res.ok) { 367→ return Response.json({ error: 'Post not found' }, { status: 404 }) 368→ } 369→ 370→ const post = await res.json() 371→ return Response.json(post) 372→} 373→``` 374→ 375→In this example, route handlers for all blog post IDs returned by `generateStaticParams` will be statically generated at build time. Requests to other IDs will be handled dynamically at request time. 376→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware.
