1→--- 2→title: layout.js 3→description: API reference for the layout.js file. 4→--- 5→ 6→The `layout` file is used to define a layout in your Next.js application. 7→ 8→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" switcher 9→export default function DashboardLayout({ 10→ children, 11→}: { 12→ children: React.ReactNode 13→}) { 14→ return
{children}
15→} 16→``` 17→ 18→```jsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.js" switcher 19→export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 20→ return
{children}
21→} 22→``` 23→ 24→In the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), `layout.js` is the outermost component in a route segment. It wraps `template.js`, `error.js`, `loading.js`, `not-found.js`, and `page.js`. 25→ 26→A **root layout** is the top-most layout in the root `app` directory. It is used to define the `` and `` tags and other globally shared UI. 27→ 28→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 29→export default function RootLayout({ 30→ children, 31→}: { 32→ children: React.ReactNode 33→}) { 34→ return ( 35→ 36→ {children} 37→ 38→ ) 39→} 40→``` 41→ 42→```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 43→export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 44→ return ( 45→ 46→ {children} 47→ 48→ ) 49→} 50→``` 51→ 52→## Reference 53→ 54→### Props 55→ 56→#### `children` (required) 57→ 58→Layout components should accept and use a `children` prop. During rendering, `children` will be populated with the route segments the layout is wrapping. These will primarily be the component of a child [Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (if it exists) or [Page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), but could also be other special files like [Loading](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) or [Error](/docs/app/getting-started/error-handling) when applicable. 59→ 60→#### `params` (optional) 61→ 62→A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) object from the root segment down to that layout. 63→ 64→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.tsx" switcher 65→export default async function Layout({ 66→ children, 67→ params, 68→}: { 69→ children: React.ReactNode 70→ params: Promise<{ team: string }> 71→}) { 72→ const { team } = await params 73→} 74→``` 75→ 76→```jsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js" switcher 77→export default async function Layout({ children, params }) { 78→ const { team } = await params 79→} 80→``` 81→ 82→| Example Route | URL | `params` | 83→| --------------------------------- | -------------- | ---------------------------------- | 84→| `app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js` | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>` | 85→| `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/layout.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` | 86→| `app/blog/[...slug]/layout.js` | `/blog/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 87→ 88→- Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 89→ - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 90→ 91→### Layout Props Helper 92→ 93→You can type layouts with `LayoutProps` to get a strongly typed `params` and named slots inferred from your directory structure. `LayoutProps` is a globally available helper. 94→ 95→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" 96→export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) { 97→ return ( 98→
99→ {props.children} 100→ {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */} 101→ {/* {props.analytics} */} 102→
103→ ) 104→} 105→``` 106→ 107→> **Good to know**: 108→> 109→> - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 110→> - After type generation, the `LayoutProps` helper is globally available. It doesn't need to be imported. 111→ 112→### Root Layout 113→ 114→The `app` directory **must** include a **root layout**, which is the top-most layout in the root `app` directory. Typically, the root layout is `app/layout.js`. 115→ 116→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 117→export default function RootLayout({ 118→ children, 119→}: { 120→ children: React.ReactNode 121→}) { 122→ return ( 123→ 124→ {children} 125→ 126→ ) 127→} 128→``` 129→ 130→```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 131→export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 132→ return ( 133→ 134→ {children} 135→ 136→ ) 137→} 138→``` 139→ 140→- The root layout **must** define `` and `` tags. 141→ - You should **not** manually add `` tags such as `` and `<meta>` to root layouts. Instead, you should use the [Metadata API](/docs/app/getting-started/metadata-and-og-images) which automatically handles advanced requirements such as streaming and de-duplicating `<head>` elements. 142→- You can create **multiple root layouts**. Any layout without a `layout.js` above it is a root layout. Two common approaches: 143→ - Using [route groups](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-groups) like `app/(shop)/layout.js` and `app/(marketing)/layout.js` 144→ - Omitting `app/layout.js` so layouts in subdirectories like `app/dashboard/layout.js` and `app/blog/layout.js` each become root layouts for their respective directories. 145→ - Navigating **across multiple root layouts** will cause a **full page load** (as opposed to a client-side navigation). 146→- The root layout can be under a **dynamic segment**, for example when implementing [internationalization](/docs/app/guides/internationalization) with `app/[lang]/layout.js`. 147→ 148→## Caveats 149→ 150→### Request Object 151→ 152→Layouts are cached in the client during navigation to avoid unnecessary server requests. 153→ 154→[Layouts](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) do not rerender. They can be cached and reused to avoid unnecessary computation when navigating between pages. By restricting layouts from accessing the raw request, Next.js can prevent the execution of potentially slow or expensive user code within the layout, which could negatively impact performance. 155→ 156→To access the request object, you can use [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) and [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) APIs in [Server Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) and Functions. 157→ 158→```tsx filename="app/shop/layout.tsx" switcher 159→import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 160→ 161→export default async function Layout({ children }) { 162→ const cookieStore = await cookies() 163→ const theme = cookieStore.get('theme') 164→ return '...' 165→} 166→``` 167→ 168→```jsx filename="app/shop/layout.js" switcher 169→import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 170→ 171→export default async function Layout({ children }) { 172→ const cookieStore = await cookies() 173→ const theme = cookieStore.get('theme') 174→ return '...' 175→} 176→``` 177→ 178→### Query params 179→ 180→Layouts do not rerender on navigation, so they cannot access search params which would otherwise become stale. 181→ 182→To access updated query parameters, you can use the Page [`searchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional) prop, or read them inside a Client Component using the [`useSearchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params) hook. Since Client Components re-render on navigation, they have access to the latest query parameters. 183→ 184→```tsx filename="app/ui/search.tsx" switcher 185→'use client' 186→ 187→import { useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation' 188→ 189→export default function Search() { 190→ const searchParams = useSearchParams() 191→ 192→ const search = searchParams.get('search') 193→ 194→ return '...' 195→} 196→``` 197→ 198→```jsx filename="app/ui/search.js" switcher 199→'use client' 200→ 201→import { useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation' 202→ 203→export default function Search() { 204→ const searchParams = useSearchParams() 205→ 206→ const search = searchParams.get('search') 207→ 208→ return '...' 209→} 210→``` 211→ 212→```tsx filename="app/shop/layout.tsx" switcher 213→import Search from '@/app/ui/search' 214→ 215→export default function Layout({ children }) { 216→ return ( 217→ <> 218→ <Search /> 219→ {children} 220→ </> 221→ ) 222→} 223→``` 224→ 225→```jsx filename="app/shop/layout.js" switcher 226→import Search from '@/app/ui/search' 227→ 228→export default function Layout({ children }) { 229→ return ( 230→ <> 231→ <Search /> 232→ {children} 233→ </> 234→ ) 235→} 236→``` 237→ 238→### Pathname 239→ 240→Layouts do not re-render on navigation, so they do not access pathname which would otherwise become stale. 241→ 242→To access the current pathname, you can read it inside a Client Component using the [`usePathname`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-pathname) hook. Since Client Components re-render during navigation, they have access to the latest pathname. 243→ 244→```tsx filename="app/ui/breadcrumbs.tsx" switcher 245→'use client' 246→ 247→import { usePathname } from 'next/navigation' 248→ 249→// Simplified breadcrumbs logic 250→export default function Breadcrumbs() { 251→ const pathname = usePathname() 252→ const segments = pathname.split('/') 253→ 254→ return ( 255→ <nav> 256→ {segments.map((segment, index) => ( 257→ <span key={index}> 258→ {' > '} 259→ {segment} 260→ </span> 261→ ))} 262→ </nav> 263→ ) 264→} 265→``` 266→ 267→```jsx filename="app/ui/breadcrumbs.js" switcher 268→'use client' 269→ 270→import { usePathname } from 'next/navigation' 271→ 272→// Simplified breadcrumbs logic 273→export default function Breadcrumbs() { 274→ const pathname = usePathname() 275→ const segments = pathname.split('/') 276→ 277→ return ( 278→ <nav> 279→ {segments.map((segment, index) => ( 280→ <span key={index}> 281→ {' > '} 282→ {segment} 283→ </span> 284→ ))} 285→ </nav> 286→ ) 287→} 288→``` 289→ 290→```tsx filename="app/docs/layout.tsx" switcher 291→import { Breadcrumbs } from '@/app/ui/Breadcrumbs' 292→ 293→export default function Layout({ children }) { 294→ return ( 295→ <> 296→ <Breadcrumbs /> 297→ <main>{children}</main> 298→ </> 299→ ) 300→} 301→``` 302→ 303→```jsx filename="app/docs/layout.js" switcher 304→import { Breadcrumbs } from '@/app/ui/Breadcrumbs' 305→ 306→export default function Layout({ children }) { 307→ return ( 308→ <> 309→ <Breadcrumbs /> 310→ <main>{children}</main> 311→ </> 312→ ) 313→} 314→``` 315→ 316→### Interaction with `loading.js` 317→ 318→Because `loading.js` sits below `layout.js` in the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), it cannot show a fallback for uncached or runtime data access in the layout itself, such as calling [`cookies()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies), [`headers()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers), or making uncached fetches. 319→ 320→The behavior depends on whether [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/caching) is enabled: 321→ 322→- **Without Cache Components:** The navigation will block until the layout finishes rendering, and the `loading.js` fallback will not be shown. 323→- **With Cache Components:** `loading.js` is treated as a regular `<Suspense>` boundary rather than a special prefetch marker. Uncached or runtime data access in the layout must be explicitly wrapped in its own `<Suspense>` boundary, otherwise Next.js guides you with a build-time error. The static shell streams immediately, and the uncached content swaps in as it resolves. 324→ 325→In both cases, to ensure instant navigation, either: 326→ 327→- Wrap runtime data access in your layout in its own `<Suspense>` boundary with a fallback. 328→- Move uncached data fetching from `layout.js` into `page.js` where `loading.js` can show a fallback. 329→ 330→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" switcher highlight={8-10} 331→import { Suspense } from 'react' 332→import { NavSkeleton } from './nav-skeleton' 333→import { DashboardNav } from './dashboard-nav' 334→ 335→export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 336→ return ( 337→ <> 338→ <Suspense fallback={<NavSkeleton />}> 339→ <DashboardNav /> 340→ </Suspense> 341→ <main>{children}</main> 342→ </> 343→ ) 344→} 345→``` 346→ 347→```jsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.js" switcher highlight={8-10} 348→import { Suspense } from 'react' 349→import { NavSkeleton } from './nav-skeleton' 350→import { DashboardNav } from './dashboard-nav' 351→ 352→export default function Layout({ children }) { 353→ return ( 354→ <> 355→ <Suspense fallback={<NavSkeleton />}> 356→ <DashboardNav /> 357→ </Suspense> 358→ <main>{children}</main> 359→ </> 360→ ) 361→} 362→``` 363→ 364→### Fetching Data 365→ 366→Layouts cannot pass data to their `children`. However, you can fetch the same data in a route more than once, and use React [`cache`](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) to dedupe the requests without affecting performance. 367→ 368→Alternatively, when using [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch)in Next.js, requests are automatically deduped. 369→ 370→```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher 371→export async function getUser(id: string) { 372→ const res = await fetch(`https://.../users/${id}`) 373→ return res.json() 374→} 375→``` 376→ 377→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" switcher 378→import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 379→import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 380→ 381→export default async function Layout({ children }) { 382→ const user = await getUser('1') 383→ 384→ return ( 385→ <> 386→ <nav> 387→ {/* ... */} 388→ <UserName user={user.name} /> 389→ </nav> 390→ {children} 391→ </> 392→ ) 393→} 394→``` 395→ 396→```jsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.js" switcher 397→import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 398→import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 399→ 400→export default async function Layout({ children }) { 401→ const user = await getUser('1') 402→ 403→ return ( 404→ <> 405→ <nav> 406→ {/* ... */} 407→ <UserName user={user.name} /> 408→ </nav> 409→ {children} 410→ </> 411→ ) 412→} 413→``` 414→ 415→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/page.tsx" switcher 416→import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 417→import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 418→ 419→export default async function Page() { 420→ const user = await getUser('1') 421→ 422→ return ( 423→ <div> 424→ <h1>Welcome {user.name}</h1> 425→ </div> 426→ ) 427→} 428→``` 429→ 430→```jsx filename="app/dashboard/page.js" switcher 431→import { getUser } from '@/app/lib/data' 432→import { UserName } from '@/app/ui/user-name' 433→ 434→export default async function Page() { 435→ const user = await getUser('1') 436→ 437→ return ( 438→ <div> 439→ <h1>Welcome {user.name}</h1> 440→ </div> 441→ ) 442→} 443→``` 444→ 445→### Accessing child segments 446→ 447→Layouts do not have access to the route segments below itself.      7→
     8→`<Link>` is a React component that extends the HTML `<a>` element to provide [prefetching](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) and client-side navigation between routes. It is the primary way to navigate between routes in Next.js.
     9→
    10→Basic usage:
    11→
    12→<AppOnly>
    13→
    14→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
    15→import Link from 'next/link'
    16→
    17→export default function Page() {
    18→  return <Link href="/dashboard">Dashboard</Link>
    19→}
    20→```
    21→
    22→```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher
    23→import Link from 'next/link'
    24→
    25→export default function Page() {
    26→  return <Link href="/dashboard">Dashboard</Link>
    27→}
    28→```
    29→
    30→</AppOnly>
    31→
    32→<PagesOnly>
    33→
    34→```tsx filename="pages/index.tsx" switcher
    35→import Link from 'next/link'
    36→
    37→export default function Home() {
    38→  return <Link href="/dashboard">Dashboard</Link>
    39→}
    40→```
    41→
    42→```jsx filename="pages/index.js" switcher
    43→import Link from 'next/link'
    44→
    45→export default function Home() {
    46→  return <Link href="/dashboard">Dashboard</Link>
    47→}
    48→```
    49→
    50→</PagesOnly>
    51→

    55→

    59→| --------------------------- | ------------------------ | ----------------- | -------- |
    60→| [`href`](#href-required)    | `href="/dashboard"`      | String or Object  | Yes      |
    61→| [`as`](#as)                 | `as="/post/abc"`         | String or Object  | -        |
    62→| [`replace`](#replace)       | `replace={false}`        | Boolean           | -        |
    63→| [`scroll`](#scroll)         | `scroll={false}`         | Boolean           | -        |
    64→| [`prefetch`](#prefetch)     | `prefetch={false}`       | Boolean           | -        |
    65→| [`shallow`](#shallow)       | `shallow={false}`        | Boolean           | -        |
    66→| [`locale`](#locale)         | `locale="fr"`            | String or Boolean | -        |
    67→| [`onNavigate`](#onnavigate) | `onNavigate={(e) => {}}` | Function          | -        |
    68→
    69→</PagesOnly>

    72→

    74→| ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------- | -------- |
    75→| [`href`](#href-required)              | `href="/dashboard"`              | String or Object | Yes      |
    76→| [`replace`](#replace)                 | `replace={false}`                | Boolean          | -        |
    77→| [`scroll`](#scroll)                   | `scroll={false}`                 | Boolean          | -        |
    78→| [`prefetch`](#prefetch)               | `prefetch={false}`               | Boolean or null  | -        |
    79→| [`onNavigate`](#onnavigate)           | `onNavigate={(e) => {}}`         | Function         | -        |
    80→| [`transitionTypes`](#transitiontypes) | `transitionTypes={['slide-in']}` | `string[]`       | -        |
    81→
    82→</AppOnly>
    83→
    84→> **Good to know**: `<a>` tag attributes such as `className` or `target="_blank"` can be added to `<Link>` as props and will be passed to the underlying `<a>` element.

## 調査結果のサマリー

Next.js App Routerに関連するドキュメントディレクトリの内容を確認しました。以下が主要な調査結果です。

### ディレクトリ構造

/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/ 配下には以下のセクションがあります：

- **01-app** - App Router関連ドキュメント（新しいルーター）
- **02-pages** - Pages Router関連ドキュメント（従来のルーター）
- **03-architecture** - アーキテクチャ関連
- **04-community** - コミュニティ関連

### App Router の主要ドキュメント（01-app配下）

#### 01-getting-started（Getting Started）
- `01-installation.md` - インストール手順
- `02-project-structure.md` - プロジェクト構造とファイル規約
- `03-layouts-and-pages.md` - レイアウトとページの作成
- `04-linking-and-navigating.md` - ページ間のリンクとナビゲーション
- `05-server-and-client-components.md` - Server/Client Components
- `06-fetching-data.md` - データ取得
- 他多数

#### 02-guides（ガイド）
- `migrating/` - マイグレーション関連
- `testing/` - テスト関連
- `upgrading/` - アップグレード関連

#### 03-api-reference（API リファレンス）

**03-file-conventions（ファイル規約）**
- `page.md` - ページコンポーネント
- `layout.md` - レイアウトコンポーネント  
- `dynamic-routes.md` - 動的ルートセグメント
- `route-groups.md` - ルートグループ
- `intercepting-routes.md` - インターセプティングルート
- `parallel-routes.md` - パラレルルート
- `loading.md` - ローディングUI
- `error.md` - エラーハンドリング
- `not-found.md` - 404ページ
- `template.md` - テンプレート
- `default.md` - デフォルトページ

**02-components（コンポーネント）**
- `link.md` - Linkコンポーネント
- `image.md` - Imageコンポーネント
- `form.md` - Formコンポーネント
- `font.md` - フォント関連
- `script.md` - スクリプト関連

### 重要な概念（確認済みドキュメント内容）

#### Page Component (`page.md`)
- ページは**Server Components**がデフォルト
- `params`と`searchParams`はPromiseで、async/awaitまたはReactの`use()`で解決
- `params`はURLの動的セグメント値を含む
- `searchParams`はクエリパラメータを含む
- 各ページはファイルシステムベースのルーティングで定義

#### Layout Component (`layout.md`)
- ページ間で共有されるUI
- ナビゲーション時に状態を保持
- Root layoutは`<html>`と`<body>`タグが必須
- 複数のRoot layoutをroute groupsで実装可能
- Layoutは再レンダリングされず、キャッシュされる
- Layoutからページへのデータ受け渡しはできない（Suspenseバウンダリで処理）

#### Dynamic Routes (`dynamic-routes.md`)
- `[slug]`形式で単一パラメータを定義
- `[...slug]`でキャッチオールセグメント
- `[[...slug]]`でオプショナルキャッチオールセグメント
- `generateStaticParams()`で静的プリレンダリング

#### Components Overview
- **Link** - クライアント側ナビゲーション、プリフェッチング機能
- その他のコンポーネント（Image、Form、Font、Script）

### 主要なファイル位置

完全なドキュメントファイル一覧：

**Getting Started:**
- `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/01-getting-started/02-project-structure.md`

- その他多数（route-groups, parallel-routes, intercepting-routes等）

**API Reference - Components:**
- `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/02-components/link.md`
- `/Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/apps/web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/02-components/image.md`

1→--- 2→title: Layouts and Pages 3→description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4→related: 5→ title: API Reference 6→ description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7→ links: 8→ - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9→ - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10→ - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11→ - app/api-reference/components/link 12→ - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13→--- 14→ 15→Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16→ 17→## Creating a page 18→ 19→A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20→ 21→ 28→ 29→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30→export default function Page() { 31→ return

Hello Next.js!

32→} 33→``` 34→ 35→```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36→export default function Page() { 37→ return

Hello Next.js!

38→} 39→``` 40→ 41→## Creating a layout 42→ 43→A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44→ 45→You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46→ 47→For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48→ 49→ 56→ 57→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58→export default function DashboardLayout({ 59→ children, 60→}: { 61→ children: React.ReactNode 62→}) { 63→ return ( 64→ 65→ 66→ {/* Layout UI */} 67→ {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68→
{children}
69→ 70→ 71→ ) 72→} 73→``` 74→ 75→```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76→export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77→ return ( 78→ 79→ 80→ {/* Layout UI */} 81→ {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82→
{children}
83→ 84→ 85→ ) 86→} 87→``` 88→ 89→The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90→ 91→## Creating a nested route 92→ 93→A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94→ 95→- `/` (Root Segment) 96→- `blog` (Segment) 97→- `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98→ 99→In Next.js: 100→ 101→- **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102→- **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103→ 104→To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105→ 106→ 113→ 114→```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 115→// Dummy imports 116→import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 117→import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 118→ 119→export default async function Page() { 120→ const posts = await getPosts() 121→ 122→ return ( 123→
    124→ {posts.map((post) => ( 125→ 126→ ))} 127→
128→ ) 129→} 130→``` 131→ 132→```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 133→// Dummy imports 134→import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 135→import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 136→ 137→export default async function Page() { 138→ const posts = await getPosts() 139→ 140→ return ( 141→
    142→ {posts.map((post) => ( 143→ 144→ ))} 145→
146→ ) 147→} 148→``` 149→ 150→You can continue nesting folders to create nested routes. For example, to create a route for a specific blog post, create a new `[slug]` folder inside `blog` and add a `page` file: 151→ 152→ 159→ 160→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 161→function generateStaticParams() {} 162→ 163→export default function Page() { 164→ return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

165→} 166→``` 167→ 168→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 169→function generateStaticParams() {} 170→ 171→export default function Page() { 172→ return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

173→} 174→``` 175→ 176→Wrapping a folder name in square brackets (e.g. `[slug]`) creates a [dynamic route segment](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) which is used to generate multiple pages from data. e.g. blog posts, product pages, etc. 177→ 178→## Nesting layouts 179→ 180→By default, layouts in the folder hierarchy are also nested, which means they wrap child layouts via their `children` prop. You can nest layouts by adding `layout` inside specific route segments (folders). 181→ 182→For example, to create a layout for the `/blog` route, add a new `layout` file inside the `blog` folder. 183→ 184→ 191→ 192→```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 193→export default function BlogLayout({ 194→ children, 195→}: { 196→ children: React.ReactNode 197→}) { 198→ return
{children}
199→} 200→``` 201→ 202→```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 203→export default function BlogLayout({ children }) { 204→ return
{children}
205→} 206→``` 207→ 208→If you were to combine the two layouts above, the root layout (`app/layout.js`) would wrap the blog layout (`app/blog/layout.js`), which would wrap the blog (`app/blog/page.js`) and blog post page (`app/blog/[slug]/page.js`). 209→ 210→## Creating a dynamic segment 211→ 212→[Dynamic segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) allow you to create routes that are generated from data. For example, instead of manually creating a route for each individual blog post, you can create a dynamic segment to generate the routes based on blog post data. 213→ 214→To create a dynamic segment, wrap the segment (folder) name in square brackets: `[segmentName]`. For example, in the `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` route, the `[slug]` is the dynamic segment. 215→ 216→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 217→export default async function BlogPostPage({ 218→ params, 219→}: { 220→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 221→}) { 222→ const { slug } = await params 223→ const post = await getPost(slug) 224→ 225→ return ( 226→
227→

230→ ) 231→} 232→``` 233→ 234→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 235→export default async function BlogPostPage({ params }) { 236→ const { slug } = await params 237→ const post = await getPost(slug) 238→ 239→ return ( 240→
241→

{post.title}

{post.content}

243→
244→ ) 245→} 246→``` 247→ 248→Learn more about [Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) and the [`params`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#params-optional) props. 249→ 250→Nested [layouts within Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#params-optional), can also access the `params` props. 251→ 252→## Rendering with search params 253→ 254→In a Server Component **page**, you can access search parameters using the [`searchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional) prop: 255→ 256→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 257→export default async function Page({ 258→ searchParams, 259→}: { 260→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 261→}) { 262→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 263→} 264→``` 265→ 266→```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 267→export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 268→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 269→} 270→``` 271→ 272→Using `searchParams` opts your page into [**dynamic rendering**](/docs/app/glossary#dynamic-rendering) because it requires an incoming request to read the search parameters from. 273→ 274→Client Components can read search params using the [`useSearchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params) hook. 275→ 276→Learn more about `useSearchParams` in [prerendered](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params#prerendering) and [dynamically rendered](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params#dynamic-rendering) routes. 277→ 278→### What to use and when 279→ 280→- Use the `searchParams` prop when you need search parameters to **load data for the page** (e.g. pagination, filtering from a database). 281→- Use `useSearchParams` when search parameters are used **only on the client** (e.g. filtering a list already loaded via props). 282→- As a small optimization, you can use `new URLSearchParams(window.location.search)` in **callbacks or event handlers** to read search params without triggering re-renders. 283→ 284→## Linking between pages 285→ 286→You can use the [`` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) to navigate between routes. `` is a built-in Next.js component that extends the HTML `` tag to provide [prefetching](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) and [client-side navigation](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#client-side-transitions). 287→ 288→For example, to generate a list of blog posts, import `` from `next/link` and pass a `href` prop to the component: 289→ 290→```tsx filename="app/ui/post.tsx" highlight={1,10} switcher 291→import Link from 'next/link' 292→ 293→export default async function Post({ post }) { 294→ const posts = await getPosts() 295→ 296→ return ( 297→
    298→ {posts.map((post) => ( 299→
  • 300→ {post.title} 301→
    • 302→ ))} 303→
304→ ) 305→} 306→``` 307→ 308→```jsx filename="app/ui/post.js" highlight={1,10} switcher 309→import Link from 'next/link' 310→ 311→export default async function Post({ post }) { 312→ const posts = await getPosts() 313→ 314→ return ( 315→
    316→ {posts.map((post) => ( 317→
  • 318→ {post.title} 319→
    • 320→ ))} 321→
322→ ) 323→} 324→``` 325→ 326→> **Good to know**: `` is the primary way to navigate between routes in Next.js. You can also use the [`useRouter` hook](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-router) for more advanced navigation. 327→ 328→## Route Props Helpers 329→ 330→Next.js exposes utility types that infer `params` and named slots from your route structure: 331→ 332→- [**PageProps**](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper): Props for `page` components, including `params` and `searchParams`. 333→- [**LayoutProps**](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper): Props for `layout` components, including `children` and any named slots (e.g. folders like `@analytics`). 334→ 335→These are globally available helpers, generated when running either `next dev`, `next build` or [`next typegen`](/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next#next-typegen-options). 336→ 337→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 338→export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 339→ const { slug } = await props.params 340→ return

Blog post: {slug}

341→} 342→``` 343→ 344→```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" 345→export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) { 346→ return ( 347→
348→ {props.children} 349→ {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */} 350→ {/* {props.analytics} */} 351→
352→ ) 353→} 354→``` 355→ 356→> **Good to know** 357→> 358→> - Static routes resolve `params` to `{}`. 359→> - `PageProps`, `LayoutProps` are global helpers — no imports required. 360→> - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 361→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. 1→--- 2→title: page.js 3→description: API reference for the page.js file. 4→--- 5→ 6→The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7→ 8→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9→export default function Page({ 10→ params, 11→ searchParams, 12→}: { 13→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15→}) { 16→ return

My Page

17→} 18→``` 19→ 20→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21→export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22→ return

My Page

23→} 24→``` 25→ 26→## Good to know 27→ 28→- The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29→- A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30→- A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31→- Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32→- In the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), `page.js` is the innermost file convention. It is wrapped by `loading.js` (Suspense boundary), `error.js` (error boundary), `template.js`, and `layout.js` in the same segment. 33→ 34→## Reference 35→ 36→### Props 37→ 38→#### `params` (optional) 39→ 40→A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 41→ 42→```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 43→export default async function Page({ 44→ params, 45→}: { 46→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 47→}) { 48→ const { slug } = await params 49→} 50→``` 51→ 52→```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 53→export default async function Page({ params }) { 54→ const { slug } = await params 55→} 56→``` 57→ 58→| Example Route | URL | `params` | 59→| ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 60→| `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 61→| `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 62→| `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 63→ 64→- Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 65→ - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 66→ 67→#### `searchParams` (optional) 68→ 69→A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 70→ 71→```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 72→export default async function Page({ 73→ searchParams, 74→}: { 75→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 76→}) { 77→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 78→} 79→``` 80→ 81→```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 82→export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 83→ const filters = (await searchParams).filters 84→} 85→``` 86→ 87→Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 88→ 89→```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 90→'use client' 91→import { use } from 'react' 92→ 93→export default function Page({ 94→ searchParams, 95→}: { 96→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 97→}) { 98→ const filters = use(searchParams).filters 99→} 100→``` 101→ 102→```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 103→'use client' 104→import { use } from 'react' 105→ 106→export default function Page({ searchParams }) { 107→ const filters = use(searchParams).filters 108→} 109→``` 110→ 111→| Example URL | `searchParams` | 112→| --------------- | ----------------------------- | 113→| `/shop?a=1` | `Promise<{ a: '1' }>` | 114→| `/shop?a=1&b=2` | `Promise<{ a: '1', b: '2' }>` | 115→| `/shop?a=1&a=2` | `Promise<{ a: ['1', '2'] }>` | 116→ 117→- Since the `searchParams` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 118→ - In version 14 and earlier, `searchParams` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 119→- `searchParams` is a **[Request-time API](/docs/app/glossary#request-time-apis)** whose values cannot be known ahead of time. Using it will opt the page into **[dynamic rendering](/docs/app/glossary#dynamic-rendering)** at request time. 120→- `searchParams` is a plain JavaScript object, not a `URLSearchParams` instance. 121→ 122→### Page Props Helper 123→ 124→You can type pages with `PageProps` to get strongly typed `params` and `searchParams` from the route literal. `PageProps` is a globally available helper. 125→ 126→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 127→export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 128→ const { slug } = await props.params 129→ const query = await props.searchParams 130→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

131→} 132→``` 133→ 134→> **Good to know** 135→> 136→> - Using a literal route (e.g. `'/blog/[slug]'`) enables autocomplete and strict keys for `params`. 137→> - Static routes resolve `params` to `{}`. 138→> - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build`, or with `next typegen`. 139→> - After type generation, the `PageProps` helper is globally available. It doesn't need to be imported. 140→ 141→## Examples 142→ 143→### Displaying content based on `params` 144→ 145→Using [dynamic route segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes), you can display or fetch specific content for the page based on the `params` prop. 146→ 147→```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 148→export default async function Page({ 149→ params, 150→}: { 151→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 152→}) { 153→ const { slug } = await params 154→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

155→} 156→``` 157→ 158→```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 159→export default async function Page({ params }) { 160→ const { slug } = await params 161→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

162→} 163→``` 164→ 165→### Handling filtering with `searchParams` 166→ 167→You can use the `searchParams` prop to handle filtering, pagination, or sorting based on the query string of the URL. 168→ 169→```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 170→export default async function Page({ 171→ searchParams, 172→}: { 173→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 174→}) { 175→ const { page = '1', sort = 'asc', query = '' } = await searchParams 176→ 177→ return ( 178→
179→

Product Listing

180→

Search query: {query}

181→

Current page: {page}

182→

Sort order: {sort}

183→
184→ ) 185→} 186→``` 187→ 188→```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 189→export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 190→ const { page = '1', sort = 'asc', query = '' } = await searchParams 191→ 192→ return ( 193→
194→

Product Listing

195→

Search query: {query}

196→

Current page: {page}

197→

Sort order: {sort}

198→
199→ ) 200→} 201→``` 202→ 203→### Reading `searchParams` and `params` in Client Components 204→ 205→To use `searchParams` and `params` in a Client Component (which cannot be `async`), you can use React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to read the promise: 206→ 207→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 208→'use client' 209→ 210→import { use } from 'react' 211→ 212→export default function Page({ 213→ params, 214→ searchParams, 215→}: { 216→ params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 217→ searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 218→}) { 219→ const { slug } = use(params) 220→ const { query } = use(searchParams) 221→} 222→``` 223→ 224→```js filename="app/page.js" switcher 225→'use client' 226→ 227→import { use } from 'react' 228→ 229→export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 230→ const { slug } = use(params) 231→ const { query } = use(searchParams) 232→} 233→``` 234→ 235→## Version History 236→ 237→| Version | Changes | 238→| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 239→| `v15.0.0-RC` | `params` and `searchParams` are now promises. A [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#150) is available. | 240→| `v13.0.0` | `page` introduced. | 241→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. 1→--- 2→title: Server and Client Components 3→description: Learn how you can use React Server and Client Components to render parts of your application on the server or the client. 4→related: 5→ title: Next Steps 6→ description: Learn more about the APIs mentioned in this page. 7→ links: 8→ - app/api-reference/directives/use-client 9→--- 10→ 11→By default, layouts and pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components), which lets you fetch data and render parts of your UI on the server, optionally cache the result, and stream it to the client. When you need interactivity or browser APIs, you can use [Client Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client) to layer in functionality. 12→ 13→This page explains how Server and Client Components work in Next.js and when to use them, with examples of how to compose them together in your application. 14→ 15→## When to use Server and Client Components? 16→ 17→The client and server environments have different capabilities. Server and Client components allow you to run logic in each environment depending on your use case. 18→ 19→Use **Client Components** when you need: 20→ 21→- [State](https://react.dev/learn/managing-state) and [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events). E.g. `onClick`, `onChange`. 22→- [Lifecycle logic](https://react.dev/learn/lifecycle-of-reactive-effects). E.g. `useEffect`. 23→- Browser-only APIs. E.g. `localStorage`, `window`, `Navigator.geolocation`, etc. 24→- [Custom hooks](https://react.dev/learn/reusing-logic-with-custom-hooks). 25→ 26→Use **Server Components** when you need: 27→ 28→- Fetch data from databases or APIs close to the source. 29→- Use API keys, tokens, and other secrets without exposing them to the client. 30→- Reduce the amount of JavaScript sent to the browser. 31→- Improve the [First Contentful Paint (FCP)](https://web.dev/fcp/), and stream content progressively to the client. 32→ 33→For example, the `` component is a Server Component that fetches data about a post, and passes it as props to the `` which handles client-side interactivity. 34→ 35→```tsx filename="app/[id]/page.tsx" highlight={1,17} switcher 36→import LikeButton from '@/app/ui/like-button' 37→import { getPost } from '@/lib/data' 38→ 39→export default async function Page({ 40→ params, 41→}: { 42→ params: Promise<{ id: string }> 43→}) { 44→ const { id } = await params 45→ const post = await getPost(id) 46→ 47→ return ( 48→

The rendering work is split into chunks, by individual route segments ([layouts and pages](/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages)): 103→ 104→- **Server Components** are rendered into a special data format called the React Server Component Payload (RSC Payload). 105→- **Client Components** and the RSC Payload are used to [prerender](/docs/app/glossary#prerendering) HTML. 106→ 107→> **What is the React Server Component Payload (RSC)?** 108→> 109→> The RSC Payload is a compact binary representation of the rendered React Server Components tree. It's used by React on the client to update the browser's DOM. The RSC Payload contains: 110→> 111→> - The rendered result of Server Components 112→> - Placeholders for where Client Components should be rendered and references to their JavaScript files 113→> - Any props passed from a Server Component to a Client Component 114→ 115→### On the client (first load) 116→ 117→Then, on the client: 118→ 119→1. **HTML** is used to immediately show a fast non-interactive preview of the route to the user. 120→2. **RSC Payload** is used to reconcile the Client and Server Component trees. 121→3. **JavaScript** is used to hydrate Client Components and make the application interactive. 122→ 123→> **What is hydration?** 124→> 125→> Hydration is React's process for attaching [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events) to the DOM, to make the static HTML interactive. 126→ 127→### Subsequent Navigations 128→ 129→On subsequent navigations: 130→ 131→- The **RSC Payload** is prefetched and cached for instant navigation. 132→- **Client Components** are rendered entirely on the client, without the server-rendered HTML. 133→ 134→## Examples 135→ 136→### Using Client Components 137→ 138→You can create a Client Component by adding the [`"use client"`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use-client) directive at the top of the file, above your imports. 139→ 140→```tsx filename="app/ui/counter.tsx" highlight={1} switcher 141→'use client' 142→ 143→import { useState } from 'react' 144→ 145→export default function Counter() { 146→ const [count, setCount] = useState(0) 147→ 148→ return ( 149→
150→

167→

170→ ) 171→} 172→``` 173→ 174→`"use client"` is used to declare a **boundary** between the Server and Client module graphs (trees). 175→ 176→Once a file is marked with `"use client"`, **all its imports and child components are considered part of the client bundle**. This means you don't need to add the directive to every component that is intended for the client. 177→ 178→### Reducing JS bundle size 179→ 180→To reduce the size of your client JavaScript bundles, add `'use client'` to specific interactive components instead of marking large parts of your UI as Client Components. 181→ 182→For example, the `` component contains mostly static elements like a logo and navigation links, but includes an interactive search bar. `` is interactive and needs to be a Client Component, however, the rest of the layout can remain a Server Component. 183→ 184→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" highlight={12} switcher 185→// Client Component 186→import Search from './search' 187→// Server Component 188→import Logo from './logo' 189→ 190→// Layout is a Server Component by default 191→export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 192→ return ( 193→ <> 194→

See an [example](/docs/app/getting-started/fetching-data#streaming-data-with-the-use-api). 288→ 289→> **Good to know**: Props passed to Client Components need to be [serializable](https://react.dev/reference/react/use-server#serializable-parameters-and-return-values) by React. 290→ 291→### Interleaving Server and Client Components 292→ 293→You can pass Server Components as a prop to a Client Component. This allows you to visually nest server-rendered UI within Client components. 294→ 295→A common pattern is to use `children` to create a _slot_ in a ``. For example, a `` component that fetches data on the server, inside a `` component that uses client state to toggle visibility. 296→ 297→```tsx filename="app/ui/modal.tsx" switcher 298→'use client' 299→ 300→export default function Modal({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { 301→ return
{children}
302→} 303→``` 304→ 305→```jsx filename="app/ui/modal.js" switcher 306→'use client' 307→ 308→export default function Modal({ children }) { 309→ return
{children}
310→} 311→``` 312→ 313→Then, in a parent Server Component (e.g.``), you can pass a `` as the child of the ``: 314→ 315→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={7} switcher 316→import Modal from './ui/modal' 317→import Cart from './ui/cart' 318→ 319→export default function Page() { 320→ return ( 321→ 322→ 323→ 324→ ) 325→} 326→``` 327→ 328→```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={7} switcher 329→import Modal from './ui/modal' 330→import Cart from './ui/cart' 331→ 332→export default function Page() { 333→ return ( 334→ 335→ 336→ 337→ ) 338→} 339→``` 340→ 341→In this pattern, all Server Components will be rendered on the server ahead of time, including those as props. The resulting RSC payload will contain references of where Client Components should be rendered within the component tree. 342→ 343→### Context providers 344→ 345→[React context](https://react.dev/learn/passing-data-deeply-with-context) is commonly used to share global state like the current theme. However, React context is not supported in Server Components. 346→ 347→To use context, create a Client Component that accepts `children`: 348→ 349→```tsx filename="app/theme-provider.tsx" switcher 350→'use client' 351→ 352→import { createContext } from 'react' 353→ 354→export const ThemeContext = createContext({}) 355→ 356→export default function ThemeProvider({ 357→ children, 358→}: { 359→ children: React.ReactNode 360→}) { 361→ return {children} 362→} 363→``` 364→ 365→```jsx filename="app/theme-provider.js" switcher 366→'use client' 367→ 368→import { createContext } from 'react' 369→ 370→export const ThemeContext = createContext({}) 371→ 372→export default function ThemeProvider({ children }) { 373→ return {children} 374→} 375→``` 376→ 377→Then, import it into a Server Component (e.g. `layout`): 378→ 379→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 380→import ThemeProvider from './theme-provider' 381→ 382→export default function RootLayout({ 383→ children, 384→}: { 385→ children: React.ReactNode 386→}) { 387→ return ( 388→ 389→ 390→ {children} 391→ 392→ 393→ ) 394→} 395→``` 396→ 397→```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 398→import ThemeProvider from './theme-provider' 399→ 400→export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 401→ return ( 402→ 403→ 404→ {children} 405→ 406→ 407→ ) 408→} 409→``` 410→ 411→Your Server Component will now be able to directly render your provider, and all other Client Components throughout your app will be able to consume this context. 412→ 413→> **Good to know**: You should render providers as deep as possible in the tree – notice how `ThemeProvider` only wraps `{children}` instead of the entire `` document. This makes it easier for Next.js to optimize the static parts of your Server Components. 414→ 415→### Third-party components 416→ 417→When using a third-party component that relies on client-only features, you can wrap it in a Client Component to ensure it works as expected. 418→ 419→For example, the `` can be imported from the `acme-carousel` package. This component uses `useState`, but it doesn't yet have the `"use client"` directive. 420→ 421→If you use `` within a Client Component, it will work as expected: 422→ 423→```tsx filename="app/gallery.tsx" switcher 424→'use client' 425→ 426→import { useState } from 'react' 427→import { Carousel } from 'acme-carousel' 428→ 429→export default function Gallery() { 430→ const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false) 431→ 432→ return ( 433→
434→ 435→ {/* Works, since Carousel is used within a Client Component */} 436→ {isOpen && } 437→
438→ ) 439→} 440→``` 441→ 442→```jsx filename="app/gallery.js" switcher 443→'use client' 444→ 445→import { useState } from 'react' 446→import { Carousel } from 'acme-carousel' 447→ 448→export default function Gallery() { 449→ const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false) 450→ 451→ return ( 452→
453→ 454→ {/* Works, since Carousel is used within a Client Component */} 455→ {isOpen && } 456→
457→ ) 458→} 459→``` 460→ 461→However, if you try to use it directly within a Server Component, you'll see an error. This is because Next.js doesn't know `` is using client-only features. 462→ 463→To fix this, you can wrap third-party components that rely on client-only features in your own Client Components: 464→ 465→```tsx filename="app/carousel.tsx" switcher 466→'use client' 467→ 468→import { Carousel } from 'acme-carousel' 469→ 470→export default Carousel 471→``` 472→ 473→```jsx filename="app/carousel.js" switcher 474→'use client' 475→ 476→import { Carousel } from 'acme-carousel' 477→ 478→export default Carousel 479→``` 480→ 481→Now, you can use `` directly within a Server Component: 482→ 483→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 484→import Carousel from './carousel' 485→ 486→export default function Page() { 487→ return ( 488→
489→

View pictures

490→ {/* Works, since Carousel is a Client Component */} 491→ 492→
493→ ) 494→} 495→``` 496→ 497→```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 498→import Carousel from './carousel' 499→ 500→export default function Page() { 501→ return ( 502→
503→

View pictures

504→ {/* Works, since Carousel is a Client Component */} 505→ 506→
507→ ) 508→} 509→``` 510→ 511→> **Advice for Library Authors** 512→> 513→> If you’re building a component library, add the `"use client"` directive to entry points that rely on client-only features. This lets your users import components into Server Components without needing to create wrappers. 514→> 515→> It's worth noting some bundlers might strip out `"use client"` directives. You can find an example of how to configure esbuild to include the `"use client"` directive in the [React Wrap Balancer](https://github.com/shuding/react-wrap-balancer/blob/main/tsup.config.ts#L10-L13) and [Vercel Analytics](https://github.com/vercel/analytics/blob/main/packages/web/tsup.config.js#L26-L30) repositories. 516→ 517→### Preventing environment poisoning 518→ 519→JavaScript modules can be shared between both Server and Client Components modules. This means it's possible to accidentally import server-only code into the client. For example, consider the following function: 520→ 521→```ts filename="lib/data.ts" switcher 522→export async function getData() { 523→ const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 524→ headers: { 525→ authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 526→ }, 527→ }) 528→ 529→ return res.json() 530→} 531→``` 532→ 533→```js filename="lib/data.js" switcher 534→export async function getData() { 535→ const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 536→ headers: { 537→ authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 538→ }, 539→ }) 540→ 541→ return res.json() 542→} 543→``` 544→ 545→This function contains an `API_KEY` that should never be exposed to the client. 546→ 547→In Next.js, only environment variables prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` are included in the client bundle. If variables are not prefixed, Next.js replaces them with an empty string. 548→ 549→As a result, even though `getData()` can be imported and executed on the client, it won't work as expected. 550→ 551→To prevent accidental usage in Client Components, you can use the [`server-only` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/server-only). 552→ 553→Then, import the package into a file that contains server-only code: 554→ 555→```js filename="lib/data.js" 556→import 'server-only' 557→ 558→export async function getData() { 559→ const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 560→ headers: { 561→ authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 562→ }, 563→ }) 564→ 565→ return res.json() 566→} 567→``` 568→ 569→Now, if you try to import the module into a Client Component, there will be a build-time error. 570→ 571→The corresponding [`client-only` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/client-only) can be used to mark modules that contain client-only logic like code that accesses the `window` object. 572→ 573→In Next.js, installing `server-only` or `client-only` is **optional**. However, if your linting rules flag extraneous dependencies, you may install them to avoid issues. 574→ 575→```bash package="npm" 576→npm install server-only 577→``` 578→ 579→```bash package="yarn" 580→yarn add server-only 581→``` 582→ 583→```bash package="pnpm" 584→pnpm add server-only 585→``` 586→ 587→```bash package="bun" 588→bun add server-only 589→``` 590→ 591→Next.js handles `server-only` and `client-only` imports internally to provide clearer error messages when a module is used in the wrong environment. The contents of these packages from NPM are not used by Next.js. 592→ 593→Next.js also provides its own type declarations for `server-only` and `client-only`, for TypeScript configurations where [`noUncheckedSideEffectImports`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#noUncheckedSideEffectImports) is active. 594→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→--- 2→title: Image Component 3→description: Optimize Images in your Next.js Application using the built-in `next/image` Component. 4→--- 5→ 6→{/* The content of this doc is shared between the app and pages router. You can use the `Content` component to add content that is specific to the Pages Router. Any shared content should not be wrapped in a component. */} 7→ 8→The Next.js Image component extends the HTML `` element for automatic image optimization. 9→ 10→```jsx filename="app/page.js" 11→import Image from 'next/image' 12→ 13→export default function Page() { 14→ return ( 15→ 21→ ) 22→} 23→``` 24→ 25→ 26→ 27→> **Good to know**: If you are using a version of Next.js prior to 13, you'll want to use the [next/legacy/image](/docs/pages/api-reference/components/image-legacy) documentation since the component was renamed. 28→ 29→ 30→ 31→## Reference 32→ 33→### Props 34→ 35→The following props are available: 36→ 37→
38→| Prop | Example | Type | Status | 39→| ----------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | --------------- | ---------- | 40→| [`src`](#src) | `src="/profile.png"` | String | Required | 41→| [`alt`](#alt) | `alt="Picture of the author"` | String | Required | 42→| [`width`](#width-and-height) | `width={500}` | Integer (px) | - | 43→| [`height`](#width-and-height) | `height={500}` | Integer (px) | - | 44→| [`fill`](#fill) | `fill={true}` | Boolean | - | 45→| [`loader`](#loader) | `loader={imageLoader}` | Function | - | 46→| [`sizes`](#sizes) | `sizes="(max-width: 768px) 100vw, 33vw"` | String | - | 47→| [`quality`](#quality) | `quality={80}` | Integer (1-100) | - | 48→| [`preload`](#preload) | `preload={true}` | Boolean | - | 49→| [`placeholder`](#placeholder) | `placeholder="blur"` | String | - | 50→| [`style`](#style) | `style={{objectFit: "contain"}}` | Object | - | 51→| [`onLoadingComplete`](#onloadingcomplete) | `onLoadingComplete={img => done())}` | Function | Deprecated | 52→| [`onLoad`](#onload) | `onLoad={event => done())}` | Function | - | 53→| [`onError`](#onerror) | `onError(event => fail()}` | Function | - | 54→| [`loading`](#loading) | `loading="lazy"` | String | - | 55→| [`blurDataURL`](#blurdataurl) | `blurDataURL="data:image/jpeg..."` | String | - | 56→| [`unoptimized`](#unoptimized) | `unoptimized={true}` | Boolean | - | 57→| [`overrideSrc`](#overridesrc) | `overrideSrc="/seo.png"` | String | - | 58→| [`decoding`](#decoding) | `decoding="async"` | String | - | 59→
60→ 61→#### `src` 62→ 63→The source of the image. Can be one of the following: 64→ 65→An internal path string. 66→ 67→```jsx 68→ 69→``` 70→ 71→An absolute external URL (must be configured with [remotePatterns](#remotepatterns)). 72→ 73→```jsx 74→ 75→``` 76→ 77→A static import. 78→ 79→```jsx 80→import profile from './profile.png' 81→ 82→export default function Page() { 83→ return 84→} 85→``` 86→ 87→> **Good to know**: For security reasons, the Image Optimization API using the default [loader](#loader) will _not_ forward headers when fetching the `src` image. 88→> If the `src` image requires authentication, consider using the [unoptimized](#unoptimized) property to disable Image Optimization. 89→ 90→#### `alt` 91→ 92→The `alt` property is used to describe the image for screen readers and search engines. It is also the fallback text if images have been disabled or an error occurs while loading the image. 93→ 94→It should contain text that could replace the image [without changing the meaning of the page](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/images.html#general-guidelines). It is not meant to supplement the image and should not repeat information that is already provided in the captions above or below the image. 95→ 96→If the image is [purely decorative](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/images.html#a-purely-decorative-image-that-doesn't-add-any-information) or [not intended for the user](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/images.html#an-image-not-intended-for-the-user), the `alt` property should be an empty string (`alt=""`). 97→ 98→> Learn more about [image accessibility guidelines](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/images.html#alt). 99→ 100→#### `width` and `height` 101→ 102→The `width` and `height` properties represent the [intrinsic](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Intrinsic_Size) image size in pixels. This property is used to infer the correct **aspect ratio** used by browsers to reserve space for the image and avoid layout shift during loading. It does not determine the _rendered size_ of the image, which is controlled by CSS. 103→ 104→```jsx 105→ 106→``` 107→ 108→You **must** set both `width` and `height` properties unless: 109→ 110→- The image is statically imported. 111→- The image has the [`fill` property](#fill) 112→ 113→If the height and width are unknown, we recommend using the [`fill` property](#fill). 114→ 115→#### `fill` 116→ 117→A boolean that causes the image to expand to the size of the parent element. 118→ 119→```js 120→ 121→``` 122→ 123→**Positioning**: 124→ 125→- The parent element **must** assign `position: "relative"`, `"fixed"`, `"absolute"`. 126→- By default, the `` element uses `position: "absolute"`. 127→ 128→**Object Fit**: 129→ 130→If no styles are applied to the image, the image will stretch to fit the container. You can use `objectFit` to control cropping and scaling. 131→ 132→- `"contain"`: The image will be scaled down to fit the container and preserve aspect ratio. 133→- `"cover"`: The image will fill the container and be cropped. 134→ 135→> Learn more about [`position`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/position) and [`object-fit`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/CSS/object-fit). 136→ 137→#### `loader` 138→ 139→A custom function used to generate the image URL. The function receives the following parameters, and returns a URL string for the image: 140→ 141→- [`src`](#src) 142→- [`width`](#width-and-height) 143→- [`quality`](#quality) 144→ 145→ 146→ 147→```js 148→'use client' 149→ 150→import Image from 'next/image' 151→ 152→const imageLoader = ({ src, width, quality }) => { 153→ return `https://example.com/${src}?w=${width}&q=${quality || 75}` 154→} 155→ 156→export default function Page() { 157→ return ( 158→ 165→ ) 166→} 167→``` 168→ 169→> **Good to know**: Using props like `onLoad`, which accept a function, requires using [Client Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client) to serialize the provided function. 170→ 171→ 172→ 173→ 174→ 175→```js 176→import Image from 'next/image' 177→ 178→const imageLoader = ({ src, width, quality }) => { 179→ return `https://example.com/${src}?w=${width}&q=${quality || 75}` 180→} 181→ 182→export default function Page() { 183→ return ( 184→ 191→ ) 192→} 193→``` 194→ 195→ 196→ 197→Alternatively, you can use the [loaderFile](#loaderfile) configuration in `next.config.js` to configure every instance of `next/image` in your application, without passing a prop. 198→ 199→#### `sizes` 200→ 201→Define the sizes of the image at different breakpoints. Used by the browser to choose the most appropriate size from the generated `srcset`. 202→ 203→```jsx 204→import Image from 'next/image' 205→ 206→export default function Page() { 207→ return ( 208→
209→ 214→
215→ ) 216→} 217→``` 218→ 219→`sizes` should be used when: 220→ 221→- The image is using the [`fill`](#fill) prop 222→- CSS is used to make the image responsive 223→ 224→If `sizes` is missing, the browser assumes the image will be as wide as the viewport (`100vw`). This can cause unnecessarily large images to be downloaded. 225→ 226→In addition, `sizes` affects how `srcset` is generated: 227→ 228→- Without `sizes`: Next.js generates a limited `srcset` (e.g. 1x, 2x), suitable for fixed-size images. 229→- With `sizes`: Next.js generates a full `srcset` (e.g. 640w, 750w, etc.), optimized for responsive layouts. 230→ 231→> Learn more about `srcset` and `sizes` on [web.dev](https://web.dev/learn/design/responsive-images/#sizes) and [mdn](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/img#sizes). 232→ 233→#### `quality` 234→ 235→An integer between `1` and `100` that sets the quality of the optimized image. Higher values increase file size and visual fidelity. Lower values reduce file size but may affect sharpness. 236→ 237→```jsx 238→// Default quality is 75 239→ 240→``` 241→ 242→If you’ve configured [qualities](#qualities) in `next.config.js`, the value must match one of the allowed entries. 243→ 244→> **Good to know**: If the original image is already low quality, setting a high quality value will increase the file size without improving appearance. ## 読み込み完了 - 分析サマリー Next.jsドキュメントと既存のコードを読み込みました。重要な破壊的変更とAPIの違いをまとめます。 ### 重要な破壊的変更 (Breaking Changes) #### 1. **`params` と `searchParams` がPromiseになった** - **Next.js 15.0.0-RC以降の変更** - 以前：同期的にアクセス可能 (`params.slug`) - 現在：Promise - `await` が必須 (`const { slug } = await params`) - クライアントコンポーネント：React.useで処理 (`use(params)`) #### 2. **Image コンポーネントの `priority` が廃止予定** - **Next.js 16以降：** `priority` が廃止され、`preload` に変更 - 例：`` を使用 - コンポーネントの使用時に注意が必要 #### 3. **ページコンポーネントのデフォルト** - ページとレイアウトはデフォルトで **Server Components** - クライアント機能が必要な場合は `'use client'` ディレクティブを追加 ### 既存コードの現在の状態 #### `/app/layout.tsx` ```tsx export default function RootLayout({ children, }: Readonly<{ children: React.ReactNode; }>) { return ( {children} ); } ``` - **現状：** Server Component（`async`ではない） - **良好な状態** - 基本的なレイアウト構造は正しい #### `/app/page.tsx` ```tsx export default function Home() { return
; } ``` - **現状：** Server Component（空のコンポーネント） - **注意点：** `params` や `searchParams` を使用する場合は Promise として処理する必要がある ### API リファレンス上の主要ポイント | 機能 | 詳細 | |------|------| | **Route Props** | `params` と `searchParams` は Promises - `async/await` または `use()` で処理 | | **Page Props Helper** | `PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>` で型安全性を確保（自動生成） | | **Dynamic Segments** | `[slug]` フォルダで動的ルート作成 | | **Server Components** | デフォルト動作 - DBアクセス・シークレット管理が可能 | | **Client Components** | `'use client'` で状態管理・イベントハンドラ・ブラウザAPI使用可能 | ### 現在のプロジェクトの確認事項 プロジェクト内の `/apps/web/AGENTS.md` に警告があります： > "This is NOT the Next.js you know - This version has breaking changes" これは現在使用しているNext.jsバージョンが15以上であること、そして `params` と `searchParams` が Promise になっているという破壊的変更に対応する必要があることを示唆しています。

# JukuBox.ai ランディングページ作成計画

## Context
apps/web プロジェクトの `app/page.tsx` が空の状態。JukuBox.ai（AIエージェント学習サービス）のランディングページを新規作成する。「レトロ × 最先端」のデザインコンセプトでJukeboxと塾のダブルミーニングを視覚化する。

## 技術的注意事項（Next.js 16 破壊的変更）
- `Image` コンポーネント: `priority` → `preload` に変更
- `params` / `searchParams` は Promise（今回は使用しない）
- ページは Server Component がデフォルト（`'use client'` 不要な場合は使わない）
- Tailwind CSS v4（`@import "tailwindcss"` 方式）

## デザインコンセプト

### カラーパレット（レトロ × 最先端）
- 背景: 深いネイビーブラック (`oklch(0.08 0.02 260)`)
- ネオンシアン: `oklch(0.85 0.18 195)` — Jukebox の光
- ネオンマゼンタ: `oklch(0.65 0.28 330)` — レトロ感
- ネオンゴールド: `oklch(0.82 0.15 85)` — 塾の権威感
- エレクトリックグリーン: `oklch(0.82 0.2 145)` — AI/テック感

### 視覚的要素
- CRT スキャンライン/グリッドオーバーレイ（レトロ感）
- ネオングロウエフェクト（box-shadow, text-shadow）
- レコード盤を模した円形装飾
- グラスモーフィズムカード（最先端感）
- アニメーション: パルス、フロート、グロウ

## ページ構成

### 1. Hero セクション
- ナビゲーションバー（ロゴ + CTA）
- 大見出し: "JukuBox.ai" （ネオンテキストエフェクト）
- サブタイトル: 「AI エージェントと好きなことを好きなだけ学ぶ」
- 説明文: 自分の AI エージェントで学習
- CTA ボタン × 2: 「無料で始める」「デモを見る」
- 背景: 暗い背景 + グリッドパターン + レコード盤装飾

### 2. Features セクション（グラスモーフィズムカード）
- 自分の AI エージェントで学ぶ
- コースを作成・共有
- 学習記録の管理
- 正解・不正解の追跡

### 3. How it works セクション
- ステップ 1: AI エージェントを接続
- ステップ 2: コースを選ぶ or 作る
- ステップ 3: 学習開始
- ステップ 4: 記録を確認

### 4. 学習記録 セクション
- いつ学習したか
- 何を学習したか
- 正解・不正解の記録
- 進捗の可視化

### 5. CTA セクション
- 「今すぐ始める」大きなボタン
- ネオンエフェクトで目立たせる

### 6. Footer
- ロゴ + キャッチコピー
- リンク

## 実装ファイル

### 変更ファイル
- `apps/web/app/globals.css` — レトロ×最先端カラー変数追加、グロウアニメーション、スキャンラインスタイル追加
- `apps/web/app/page.tsx` — ランディングページ全体（Server Component）

### 新規作成ファイル（必要に応じて）
- `apps/web/app/_components/hero.tsx` — Heroセクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/features.tsx` — Featuresセクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/how-it-works.tsx` — How it worksセクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/learning-records.tsx` — 学習記録セクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/cta.tsx` — CTAセクション

## 検証方法
1. `make dev-web`（または `pnpm dev`）で開発サーバー起動
2. ブラウザで http://localhost:3000 を確認
3. playwright-cli でスクリーンショット取得
4. ブラウザコンソールにエラーがないことを確認
5. レスポンシブ確認（モバイル・タブレット・デスクトップ）

- ネオンマゼンタ: `oklch(0.65 0.28 330)` — レトロ感
- ネオンゴールド: `oklch(0.82 0.15 85)` — 塾の権威感
- エレクトリックグリーン: `oklch(0.82 0.2 145)` — AI/テック感

### 視覚的要素
- CRT スキャンライン/グリッドオーバーレイ（レトロ感）
- ネオングロウエフェクト（box-shadow, text-shadow）
- レコード盤を模した円形装飾
- グラスモーフィズムカード（最先端感）
- アニメーション: パルス、フロート、グロウ

## ページ構成

### 1. Hero セクション
- ナビゲーションバー（ロゴ + CTA）
- 大見出し: "JukuBox.ai" （ネオンテキストエフェクト）
- サブタイトル: 「AI エージェントと好きなことを好きなだけ学ぶ」
- 説明文: 自分の AI エージェントで学習
- CTA ボタン × 2: 「無料で始める」「デモを見る」
- 背景: 暗い背景 + グリッドパターン + レコード盤装飾

### 2. Features セクション（グラスモーフィズムカード）
- 自分の AI エージェントで学ぶ
- コースを作成・共有
- 学習記録の管理
- 正解・不正解の追跡

### 3. How it works セクション
- ステップ 1: AI エージェントを接続
- ステップ 2: コースを選ぶ or 作る
- ステップ 3: 学習開始
- ステップ 4: 記録を確認

### 4. 学習記録 セクション
- いつ学習したか
- 何を学習したか
- 正解・不正解の記録
- 進捗の可視化

### 5. CTA セクション
- 「今すぐ始める」大きなボタン
- ネオンエフェクトで目立たせる

### 6. Footer
- ロゴ + キャッチコピー
- リンク

## 実装ファイル

### 変更ファイル
- `apps/web/app/globals.css` — レトロ×最先端カラー変数追加、グロウアニメーション、スキャンラインスタイル追加
- `apps/web/app/page.tsx` — ランディングページ全体（Server Component）

### 新規作成ファイル（必要に応じて）
- `apps/web/app/_components/hero.tsx` — Heroセクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/features.tsx` — Featuresセクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/how-it-works.tsx` — How it worksセクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/learning-records.tsx` — 学習記録セクション
- `apps/web/app/_components/cta.tsx` — CTAセクション

## 検証方法
1. `make dev-web`（または `pnpm dev`）で開発サーバー起動
2. ブラウザで http://localhost:3000 を確認
3. playwright-cli でスクリーンショット取得
4. ブラウザコンソールにエラーがないことを確認
5. レスポンシブ確認（モバイル・タブレット・デスクトップ）

JukuBox.ai のランディングページを作成してください。

## サービス情報
- サービス名: JukuBox.ai
- キャッチコピー: 「AIエージェントと好きなことを好きなだけ学ぶ」
- ダブルミーニング: 日本語の「塾」+ 音楽が流せる「Jukebox」
- サービス概要: 自分が契約しているAIエージェントを使って学習できるサービス

## サービスの主な機能
- 自分の契約しているAIエージェントを使って学習
- 自分でコースを作成することも、他の人が作ったコースを使うことも可能
- 学習進捗・日時・内容の記録
- 正解・不正解の記録

## デザイントーン
- Retro × Cutting-Edge（レトロ × 最先端）の融合
- Jukebox のレトロなイメージ（ネオンサイン、レコード、アーケード感）
- AIエージェントの最先端イメージ（グラスモーフィズム、グラデーション）
- 暗い背景にネオンカラーのアクセント
- CRTスキャンライン、グリッドパターン（レトロ感）
- グロウエフェクト、ネオングロウ

## カラーパレット（CSS変数として globals.css に追加）

```css
--juku-bg: oklch(0.08 0.02 260);
--juku-neon-cyan: oklch(0.85 0.18 195);

--juku-neon-gold: oklch(0.82 0.15 85);
--juku-neon-green: oklch(0.82 0.2 145);
--juku-surface: oklch(0.12 0.03 260 / 0.8);
```

## 技術スタック
- Next.js 16 (App Router, Server Components デフォルト)
- Tailwind CSS v4 (`@import "tailwindcss"` 方式)
- shadcn (base-mira スタイル)

- `Image` コンポーネント: `priority` プロパティは廃止、代わりに `preload` を使う
- ページは Server Component がデフォルト
- インタラクティブな要素（useState等）は `'use client'` が必要

### 新規作成: `apps/web/app/_components/features.tsx`
グラスモーフィズムカード × 4:

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/haru/ghq/github.com/harusame0616/ijuku/.claude/skills/frontend-design

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

The user provides frontend requirements: a component, page, application, or interface to build. They may include context about the purpose, audience, or technical constraints.

## Design Thinking

Before coding, understand the context and commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work - the key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue, etc.) that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

Focus on:
- **Typography**: Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Avoid generic fonts like Arial and Inter; opt instead for distinctive choices that elevate the frontend's aesthetics; unexpected, characterful font choices. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
- **Color & Theme**: Commit to a cohesive aesthetic. Use CSS variables for consistency. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.
- **Motion**: Use animations for effects and micro-interactions. Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML. Use Motion library for React when available. Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay) creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.
- **Spatial Composition**: Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements. Generous negative space OR controlled density.
- **Backgrounds & Visual Details**: Create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to solid colors. Add contextual effects and textures that match the overall aesthetic. Apply creative forms like gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors, and grain overlays.

NEVER use generic AI-generated aesthetics like overused font families (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system fonts), cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients on white backgrounds), predictable layouts and component patterns, and cookie-cutter design that lacks context-specific character.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.

**IMPORTANT**: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back, show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.
